Sites

Environmental storytelling
Running sites
Exploring sites
Creating sites
The Green Lord’s Tomb

345

Sites

Sites are interesting places that the PCs
can explore. They might be the destination
of an expedition, a point of interest along
the way, or just an incidental discovery.
They are places where adventure happens!
Sites vary greatly in size and scope. Some
are very small, discrete places like a stone
altar on a lonely hilltop. Some are made
up of distinct rooms, areas, or zones—like
a cave system, or a barrow mound, or a
crumbling manor from the age of the
Makers. Some are sprawling complexes
made up of multiple smaller sites.
346

Most sites are out in the wilderness,
but you can think of a settlement (like
Marshedge or Gordin’s Delve or a farming
valley up in the Whitefang Mountains) as
a site as well. You can even find sites within
settlements: the Judge’s Chronicle (if there
is one), the cistern in Stonetop (if there’s
more down there than water), the mill in
Marshedge, the titular delves of Gordin’s
Delve—these are all sites!

Sites

Not every place where you frame a scene
needs to be a “site” per se. Many locations
are merely the backdrop to a scene, a place
where you present a danger or a discovery
or where the PCs interact with someone.
The creek that the PCs need to cross isn’t
a site, it’s just a hazard. The wayside on the
road is just a landmark, or a place where
the PCs meet some travelers.

contrast with the safe and familiar places
back home, and thus punctuate the PCs’
lives with adventure.

So, what distinguishes a site from just “a
place where you frame a scene?”

Meaningful decisions are about more than
just navigation. Even a simple, linear site
can be full of choices! Do the PCs try to
talk to the ghost who haunts this place, or
avoid it, or bind it, or what? Do they futz
with the magic runes or leave them be?

A site should tell a story, even if you're
making that story up on the fly. You want
your players asking, "What happened
here?" and, "Why is this like this?" and
it should feel like there's an answer, even
if it's beyond their reach. When your site
tells a story, you’re portraying a rich and
mysterious world.
Sites should be exciting places to explore:
mysterious, wonderous, often dangerous.
They should stir your players’ curiosity and
keep everyone a little on edge. They should

Finally, sites should present players with
meaningful decisions. Not just “do we go
left or right?” but “do we follow the footprints down this tunnel, or check out this
side passage that smells of ozone?”

When they fall into the icy water, do they
press further into the cave, wet and shivery
and risking hypothermia? Or do they spend
time and torches drying their clothes?
Create sites that are dynamic and unstable,
full of potential energy like a coiled spring.
Then see what the PCs decide to do, and
play to find out what happens.
347

Sites

Environmental
storytelling
Sites have stories to tell. You should be
able to answer questions like:
		 Who built this place?
		 How did it fall into ruin or disuse?
		 Who or what dwells here now?
		 When and why did they arrive?
		 What are they up to now?
		 What else has happened here?
The procedures later in this chapter (page
355) will help you establish the site’s
story, as will the resources in Book II: The
Wider World and Other Wonders.
In play, you tell a site’s story through its
nouns—through the rooms or areas you
present, the way you describe the environment, and the dangers and discoveries you
put in front of the PCs.
For example, let’s say your site was a Stone
Lord temple to Aratis, then perverted by
the Barrow Builders, and later sealed away
by the Hillfolk. When the PCs explore it,
they might find a crude stone slab, carved
with warnings and runes. If they pried it
open, they’d find a vast, dusty hall, lined
with marred icons of Aratis. Further in: a
blood-stained altar, discarded bones, and
the tormented ghosts of sacrificial victims.
Now, what if a sorcerer has recently been
here to claim the site’s power? Then that
stone slab would be ajar. The sorcerer’s
footprints might be visible in the dust. If
the PCs approached the altar, they’d smell
the rotting corpse of a recent victim. If
they weren’t careful, they’d get attacked by
some unnatural vermin, drawn to the offal.
348

"What here tells you...?"
Consider asking your players to paint
the scene for you. Identify a detail or
element of the site’s story and ask,
“What here tells you __?” Let them
do the environmental storytelling.
For example, “What here tells you
that…
… this place has been sealed up for
centuries?”
… this was once a shrine to Aratis?”
… that the shrine was defiled and
used for human sacrifice?”
This approach engages players, but
don’t overuse it. Save it for when you
know what you want to establish, but
you don’t care about the specifics and
you’re happy to build on what the
players give you.
This technique isn’t for everyone. It
blurs the Line (page 195) and some
players will feel like you’re asking
them to do your job. Others might
freeze up, or introduce zany details
that don’t fit. But with the right
group, it can be magical.
In each room, area, or scene, try to include
at least one detail or element that reflects
the site’s story. Even if your players don’t
pick up on it, or they never piece the story
together, that sort of detail will make the
site feel richer and more mysterious.

Sites

Running sites

When the PCs explore a site, you run the
game normally and follow the core loop
(page 169). You play out a series of scenes
and situations, as always. They just happen
to take place in that site.

Considerations

As PCs explore sites—as you establish the
situation, make GM moves, and resolve
PC actions—keep in mind:
The physical space
Its rough dimensions, its construction, the
height of ceilings and width of passages, obstacles, footing, slopes, different levels. You
can use maps to help describe and clarify
the site’s layout and where things are.
Light sources
Who’s carrying them? What are their tags?
How far can they see? What out in the
dark can see them? How long will their
light sources last, and is that long enough?
Senses other than sight
Sounds of course, but also odors and
textures, heat and cold and humidity, air
pressure and movement.
Marching order
Who’s going first, who’s next, and who’s
bringing up the rear? This is especially
important in cramped quarters or when
something attacks.
Equipment
What does everyone have in hand, or at
the ready? How heavy a load are they carrying? If it’s a heavy load, how much does
that suck? Do they have room to swing
that awkward maul in here? Can they fit
through that tunnel with their shield?

Followers and NPCs
Include them in the situation! Where are
they? What are they doing? How do they
react to __? Make GM moves through them,
or at them. If left behind at camp or to stand
guard, what (if anything) befalls them?
Physical and mental states
Hunger and thirst, fatigue, irritability, disorientation, dread. How do the PCs react? How
do NPCs handle it? (Spoiler: not well.)
Denizens
Who or what else is here? What signs of
their presence might the PCs find? Would
they notice the PCs? If so, how would they
react? Are they hostile or wary or curious,
or what?

Light sources & tags
Here’s a quick reference:
Hand: E.g., a rushlight. Illuminates
no farther than your reach. Barely
enough to navigate or read by.
Close, area: E.g., a candle, oil lamp, or
lantern. Casts light a few feet,
enough for 2 people to navigate by.
Reach, area: E.g., a torch. Illuminates
out to ~10 feet, enough for a
small party to navigate by.
Crude: E.g., a rushlight, an oil lamp.
Prone to flicker or snuff out when
moved quickly or in light wind.
Dangerous: E.g., a torch. A big open
flame, smoky, prone to catch
things on fire if not careful.
Slow: E.g., a tinderbox. It takes at least
a few minutes to start a new fire!
Lamp oil is useless as a weapon. It
takes time and effort to light, doesn’t
burn very hot, and goes out if spilled.
349

Sites

Exploring sites
Most sites are made up of multiple discrete
areas or rooms. Some sites are so big that
these areas or rooms are divided into zones
or smaller sites.
As the PCs explore a site, you’ll zoom in
on one of these discrete places, establish
a scene or situation there, and play it out.
When the PCs move on, you transition
to the next interesting scene or situation.
But how do you decide what and where
that is?
If you haven’t already done so, establish
the PCs’ intent. Which way do they go?
What are they looking for? What are they
trying to escape or avoid? If their intent
isn’t clear, ask questions until it is.
Once you’ve established the PCs’ intent,
consult your prep, the appropriate entries
in Book II, and/or your growing sense of
this site, its story, and its contents. If the
next interesting place is obvious, great.
Frame the action there.
During the first adventure, the PCs
have stopped to investigate an empty
crinwin nest, high up in a great big
tree. They’ve already searched the brush
nearby and found a few dead crinwin
that seem to have killed each other.
They’ve learned all they can down here
and are talking about moving on. But
Caradoc looks up at the empty nest and
says “I want to check that out.” To do so,
he’ll need to climb the tree—which my
notes say is like a sequoia redwood! That
will be a challenge for sure, and thus
where I’ll frame the next scene.

350

For larger sites with many areas or rooms,
you might rely on a player move to inform
where you frame things next. For example,
the PCs might…
… Defy Danger to find the specific place
(the danger being they get lost and/or
stumble into a danger);
… Seek Insight to explore the site and see
what they find;
… Know Things about a place they’ve
studied, to know where __ might be;
… use a playbook move, like Expert
Tracker (page 130) to follow a foe’s
trail to wherever it leads; or…
… use a custom move specific to this site
(like the Seek Out move from The
Deeps, Book II, page 251).
If no obvious place presents itself, then
pick a GM move you want to make. Then
pick a place in the site where it would
make sense for you to make that move, or
create such a place on the fly.
If you need inspiration at any point in this
process, refer to your prep. If that doesn’t
help, pick or roll from a relevant terrain,
discovery, or site table in Book II, or use an
example site (tweaking it as you see fit).
Once you’ve decided where to establish
the next scene or situation, transition to it.
That could be as simple as just framing the
action. Something like:
		 “You enter the next room and see…”
		 “You follow the outline of the broken
road further into the ruins. It’s been
like half an hour when…”
		 “You run headlong into the tunnels,
who knows how long, and when you
stop to catch your breath, you find
yourself…”

Sites

But if the next interesting room or area
is some distance away, you might want
to spend a little play-time getting there.
Zoom out and handle it like a leg of travel
(page 312) on an expedition:
		 Describe the environment that the PCs
pass through
		 Ask the PCs questions (about how
they’re feeling, what’s on their mind,
what this makes them think of, etc.)
		 Portray followers and other NPCs
		Maybe Keep Company

You might make a soft GM move, too. Use
up their resources as they march (“Mark
off another hour on your lamp oil”). Hint
at more than meets the eye (“That’s the
third time you’ve seen that mark”). Point
to a looming danger (“A bit later, you
find these gouges, like from the talons of
something really big”).
Or, to heck with it. Introduce a danger or
present a discovery at some point along
the way, and I guess this is where the next
scene happens, actually.

The PCs and Andras are at Three Coven
Lake, atop the dam and surveying the
sprawling ruins. I present a choice of paths:
climb the bluff to the north and check out the
rooms carved into it, or explore the gentler
southern shore. They stay clear of the water
and go north.

Blodwen checks in with Andras, who plays
it off as no big deal. Then I get back to my
description. “A bit later, the stairs turn and
run up the side of the bluff. There’s no railing, just a sheer drop to your left, down to
rocks and more water than you’ve ever seen.
What are each of you thinking right now?”

I haven’t had time to fully prep, so, I skim the
Three Coven Lake entry. There’s lots of terrain and smaller sites I could pick from, but
that’s not inherently interesting. I decide to
introduce a danger, person, or faction. I roll
an encounter (Book II, page 443). I get a
6 (“other entities”) and then pick “Stone Lord
constructs.” I roll a disposition of “curious.”
I think a lithic servant is spying on the PCs
(on behalf of some other entity?). I’m not
sure where this encounter will occur, so I look
at the bluffs terrain table (Book II, page
441) and roll an “overhang” and “walkway/
stairs… carved into the stone.”

After the players answer, I zoom in to make
my move. “After like an hour, the stairs pass
through a short tunnel in an outcrop. And
Caradoc, something… moves. Up on top of
that outcrop, like 30 feet up. It’s a… person?
No… more like a man-sized puppet made
of stone, watching you. What do you do?”

Okay, cool, but I think it’ll take a bit of a hike
to get there. They set a marching order, and I
start transitioning to the scene. “So, you head
up into the bluffs, climbing this crumbling,
rock-strewn stairway. It’s a slog, and Andras
keeps wincing on his leg.”

“This is no good,” says Rhianna. “I Read the
Land. What’s the best spot for an ambush?
I want to catch this thing.” We decide that
they could draw it into a chamber in the
bluffs that Rhianna spots up ahead—and
that’s where we frame the next scene!

The party stops. Vahid Knows Things (it’s a
Stone Lord construct, but he’s unsure if it’s a
drone or autonomous or what). They try to
communicate but it doesn’t respond. Caradoc
tries climbing up to it, but it slinks away.
They move on, but it follows at a distance.

351

Sites

GM exploration moves

Use these GM moves heavily as the PCs
explore a site. These are the same moves
that you use while running a journey
(page 312), but here we’ll discuss using
them in more local, focused areas.
Your exploration moves are:
ä		 Provide a choice of paths
ä		 Hint at more than meets the eye
ä		 Offer riches at a price
ä		 Present a discovery
ä		 Point to a looming danger
ä		 Introduce a danger, person, or faction
ä		 Bar the way
ä Provide a choice of paths
Use this move to prompt the PCs to move
on, signaling that there’s nothing more
of interest here. Use to it remind them of
their options when they decide to move on
themselves. Or, use it to force a decision.
The choice of paths should be meaningful.
Know where each path leads (at least in
general) before you make this move, and
give the players some indication of how
they’re different.
“Passages go left and right. Both curve
inward, like so. To the left, at the end of
your light, you see rubble. To the right,
you can make out an alcove in the inner
wall. And ahead of you, you’re not sure
but you think it opens to a big, vaulted
room. Which way do you go?”
“So, what do you think? Do you want to
head north and take those stairs up into
the bluffs? Or pick your way down to
the south shore and check out those ruins
poking out of the lake? Or what?”
352

ä Hint at more than meets the eye
Think about something you want to reveal
to the PCs, but that wouldn’t be obvious.
Then think about what telltale signs could
point to it. Describe that. See what the
PCs do. They’ll often ask questions, Know
Things, or Seek Insight—or all three!
This move is a great way to telegraph a
discovery—especially a clue (page 424)
or an opportunity (page 428)—and to
engage in environmental storytelling.
“While Vahid and Caradoc bicker about
the rubble, Rhianna, you notice these
smooth, curving patterns in the dirt.
What do you do?”
“Vahid, now that you’re closer, you can
see that both have the same sigil carved
into their, well, ‘foreheads,’ I guess?
Stone Lord markings, for sure, and…
they look kind of familiar?”
“Okay, so Blodwen, you’re patching
up Caradoc. I presume you’ve got the
lantern on the cave floor next to you?
Well, as you finish, you notice the flame
like, pulling, towards the back of the
cave. Like there’s a draft, maybe? What
do you do?”

Sites

ä Offer riches at a price
Present something valuable to the PCs—
be it actual treasure, information, a needed
supply, etc.—but also present a danger that
makes it costly or difficult to get. See if
they think it's worth it.
“There’s this beautiful wooden chest up
against the wall, covered in soil and
dirt but still intact, about yea wide. And
there’s a dead crinwin in front of it,
clutching a withered, blackened arm.”
“ Yeah, Vahid, these are Tempest Lord
runes. Not only that, this is some sort of
formula or spell, related to… thunder? But you can’t tell more than that
without digging it up, which will take
time and make noise. You’ll risk a run-in
with that beast whose scat Caradoc
found earlier. What do you do?"
ä Present a discovery
Pick a discovery—a clue, an encounter, an
opportunity, an artifact or arcanum, or even
another, smaller site. Maybe roll on a table
from Book II to decide what it is. Put it in
front of the PCs, and see what they do. Refer to the Discoveries chapter (page 421)
for guidance and more examples.
“As you slink about the courtyard, your
foot hits something hard. Metal? You
look closer, and yeah, it’s a bronze slab,
poking out of the soil. And, oh-ho, there
are more runes on it! What do you do?”
“‘Hey, guys?’ says Andras. He’s looking
at a wall in the back, and when you get
closer you see it’s a map. Of the whole
city, from back before it fell. Here, let me
sketch it out. It looks like… this.”

ä Point to a looming danger
Use this move to telegraph a threat, a
monster, or a hazard, to reveal and reflect
its presence in the site.
“What should you be on the lookout for?
Um… oh. You find some scat, like fistsized, and ropey, full of fur and bones.
Whatever this is from, it’s a predator. A
big one. What’s your next question?”
“So, Vahid, you’re checking out that side
passage? Cool. As you approach, you see
that it’s blocked by a solid slab of stone.
And then there’s this eerie, like, ‘whoosh’
and everyone’s lanterns sputter. You all
feel a chill, and Vahid, your breath is
fogging up. What do you do?"
ä Introduce a danger, person,
or faction
Put a danger directly in the PCs’ way. As
a hard move, have it catch them unawares.
Or, have them encounter a denizen of the
site, or a group thereof. The line between
“person” and “danger” isn’t always clear.
“Standing by the pool, so still you don’t
even notice him at first, is Wynfor. He
didn’t look up when you entered. Still
hasn’t. He’s just… staring at the water,
eyes glassy, with this little half-smile.
He's drugged, maybe? What do you do?”
“A 2? Huh. Well. You step closer to the
barrow entrance, but then URK—your
foot’s caught on something. You look
down and see a reddish vine, coiled
around your ankle and calf, covered
in inch-long thorns, some of which are
stuck in you. But you barely feel it. What
do you do?"
353

Sites

ä Bar the way
Make this move to deny access to part of
a site. It could be something that’s been
there all along, like a locked door or a
chasm. Or you could cut the PCs off as
they explore, via a cave-in, a trap, a bridge
collapsing behind them, etc.
Maybe there’s a key they can find, or a way
through with a lot of effort, or another,
riskier path?
Or not. Sometimes the way is shut, and
they have to turn back for now.
“No, the slab is solid, and probably a
foot or two thick? It’s weird, though.
This very much feels like an entrance.
The way it’s inset into the construction
around it… did this, like, fall into place?
Regardless, you don’t see any way to lift
it up again. What do you do?”
“What’s the best way through? Well, not
that way. The passage is just gone, like
rubble and rock and dust floating in
the air. If you want to rejoin the others,
you’ll need to loop around, back through
the shrine and the central chamber.
What do you do?”

Use other moves, too!
Don’t forget to use your standard
GM moves while the PCs explore
a site, as well as threat moves (page
284) and moves from hazards and
monsters. For example:
ä		 Announce trouble: “You hear
footsteps approaching.”
ä		 Use up their resources: “It’s been
an hour: mark off your light
sources.”
ä		 Demonstrate a downside: “Your
axe catches on the low ceiling, and the thing darts in and
chomps your leg for 1d8 damage.”
ä		 Separate them: “Too slow, I
guess—you tumble into the tunnel below, alone. Oh, and take
1d8 damage.”
ä		 Tell them the consequences/
requirements and ask: “You’ll
have to Defy Danger, and you
risk a nasty, bone-breaking fall;
do you do it?”
ä		 Advance towards impending
doom: “It’s no use! You’re caught
in the swirling current and can’t
swim free—the whirlpool is getting closer!”
ä		 Hurt them: “ZZZOLT, there’s a
flash of light and pain and now
you’re slumped against the wall,
singed and aching—take 1d6+2
damage, ignores armor, and
marked dazed.”

354

Sites

Creating sites

You won’t always create sites the same way,
or to the same level of detail. Sometimes,
you’ll improvise a site in play; other times,
you’ll carefully prep them between sessions.
Often, you’ll do a bit of both.
Regardless of how much you’re prepping
vs. improvising, you want your site to tell
a story, to be an exciting place to explore,
and to present the players with plenty of
meaningful decisions.
Here's how to do that:
1) Lay the foundation
		 Ask questions
		 Build on what you’ve got
		Exploit the setting guide
2) Build up its story
		 Look for connections
		 Ask yourself questions
		 Answer those questions
		 Create a timeline
3) Sketch out the site’s contents
		 Populate it
		Identify dangers and discoveries
		 Establish areas/rooms
		 Describe the environment
		 Arrange areas/rooms
4) Write it up as much as you like
		 Create a map or visual (maybe)
		 Detail each area/room
		 Subdivide as needed
		 Make lists and tables
		 Make plans
		 Review and revise

You don’t need to do this entire process,
or do it all at once. If pressed for time, do
only as much of the process as you need
for the next session. Improvise the rest, and
finish later if it’s relevant.
Last session, the PCs lured a curious
construct into a site north of Three Coven
Lake. I laid the foundation during play,
picking an example site from Book II:
“warren of rooms carved into a bluff.
Once a Stone Lord’s manor, then a
warlord’s fortress, finally the site of her
demise.” I made up details as the PCs set
their ambush, and we ended there.
Between sessions, I flesh out this warren
they’ve found themselves in, building up
its story and sketching out some contents.
I suspect that the PCs will make this
their base camp, so I even draw a map.
I still need to decide what this construct’s
deal is, and figure out where in Three
Coven Lake the PCs might find the
Mindgem’s fiery heart (the whole point
of this expedition). I bet they’re related! I
lay the foundation for the heart’s resting
place: a reclaimed tenement north of the
bluffs, home to a deathless sorcerer whose
mind is spread among a number of lithic
servants (all powered by the fiery heart).
I write up the sorcerer, but don’t bother
detailing the site yet—I doubt the PCs
will get there next session.
Instead, I create a few other areas the PCs
might encounter first: a haunted workshop, a broken obsidian mirror, and the
home of an exiled Hillfolk spirit-talker
(the sorcerer’s rival). I’ll flesh out the
sorcerer’s complex after the next session.

355

Sites

1 		Foundation
						Start with a core concept for the
site and its story, something with a few
compelling details that you can build on
while improvising or as you continue to
prep. This should be brief: a few sentences
and an evocative title, no more.
Ask questions
If you’re establishing the site during play,
and the PCs might know about it, defer
to them. Has a PC been here before? Ask
what it’s like! Would a PC have heard of
it? Ask what they’ve heard.
As always, incorporate the players’ answers
into the fiction but feel free to put a twist
on it. Sure, that barrow was full of bones
and swarming mice when the Fox explored
it, but that was years ago and the mice
could be gone. Sure, everyone knows the
ruin is haunted, but perhaps it’s a grumpy
Fae who dwells there rather than a ghost.
For our first adventure, the main threat
(Sajra the swyn, a monkey-headed snake
beast) dwells in a Green Lord tomb. The
crinwin are dragging Pryder (one of the
village youths) back there, and the PCs
are in pursuit. I’ve already Charted a
Course and prepped the journey, and I
expect the PCs to arrive next session.
Earlier, we established that Rhianna
thinks the crinwin are headed to a big
Maker-ruin. She and her pa followed
a rabid wolf there in her youth. They
found it near the ruin, but got spooked.
“What spooked you off ?” I asked. This
was before the Forest Folk left, and she
said they’d found the wolf ’s burnt bones
near the entrance, like an offering, and
they wanted nothing to do with that!
356

Build on what you’ve got
Think through what you know about this
site. What can you assume from context?
What have you told the players and what
have they told you? What have you already
decided, in your heart of hearts?
If there’s a resource that you want to build
on or adapt—like an image or map, a module from another RPG, or an adventure
starter (page 22)—think about that, too!
Why are you putting this site in the game?
For color? To break up a journey? Because
this is where the PCs can find what they’re
looking for? Or what? Do your reasons
imply anything more about this site?
If you’re prepping in advance, then write
this stuff down. If improvising, just think
it through. Either way: let your creativity
build on this foundation.
I jot down what I know about this site:
•		 1½ days away, in the Great Wood
•		 Sajra’s been here since last fall
•		 Sajra displaced a Fae squatter,
Thornthumb, who’s still in the area
and holding a grudge
•		 Sajra has entranced a nest of
crinwin and two Stonetop children
•		 Crinwin are looting a nearby ruin to
add to Sajra’s hoard
•		 Rhianna was here in her youth
•		 Forest Folk left burnt offerings (?)
I'm putting this site in the game because
I need a lair for the villain, and because
I'd like the PCs to meet a mummified
Green Lord, maybe. Which tells me that:
•		 The tomb is basically intact

Sites

Exploit the setting guide
Book II is full of example sites and tools
to help you create your own. Use it for
inspiration and to help make sites that fit
the larger Stonetop setting.
If you're making a site from scratch, start
by deciding what manner of site it is: a Fae
domain, a ruin of the Makers, a haunted
site, etc. If unsure, refer to the entry for the
relevant region and review the sections for
“Terrain” and/or “Discoveries” therein.
Once you know the manner of site you're
creating, go to the “Sites” section for the
appropriate entry. For example, you’ll find
the entry for Forge Lord sites on page 158
of Book II, and haunted sites on page 72,
in the “Death and the undying” entry. Use
the procedures and tables there to develop
the site's concept.
Treat the tables as creative prompts. Pick
options that make sense or catch your
eye, or roll if unsure. If you do roll, try to
make sense of the results even if they seem
disjointed. Unlikely combinations can lead
to some of the most interesting, memorable sites. That said, if a roll really doesn't
feel right, then pick something else or roll
again. You're not beholden to the dice!
Consider the results in light of what you’ve
already established. Envision the site. If
you’re prepping in advance, write a few
sentences describing the place. Try to get
fairly specific, but keep it brief.
If you're creating the site on the fly, you
can ask your players to roll the dice. Read
the results out loud, or write them down
and “Hmm” sagely, revealing them through
play. Either way, take a short break to think
through the rest of the process.

The swyn's lair is a Green Lord tomb,
so I find the “Sites” section of the Green
Lord entry. This is a “ruin,” so I start
there (Book II, page 214). The process
has me roll or pick a Green Lord theme,
and “tombs, mummification, constructed
afterlives” is the obvious choice. I roll a 6
for structure, “ziggurat/pyramid/dome”
and the purpose is clearly a “tomb.” I roll
a 1 for architectural elements, “huge slabs
of smooth stone…carved with scrollwork
and bas relief.” Because I want a Green
Lord mummy to still be in there, I choose
“buried, submerged, and/or overgrown,
but mostly intact” for the site’s condition.
The procedure then has me pick a Great
Wood terrain, so I flip to that table and
roll a 4, "clearing, meadow, sparse trees."
It also says to populate it with lingering signs, artifacts, and dangers but I'll
worry about that later.
I ponder a bit and write down:
THE GREEN LORD’S TOMB
A dome-shaped tomb, mostly intact,
with a few old oaks growing out of
it. Looks like a small hill, with bits of
worked stone poking out of the soil.
Inside are wide-open halls of white
marble with intricate scrollwork,
leading to the still-intact burial
vault of a Green Lord. Forest Folk
used to leave it offerings. A Fae,
Thornthumb, used to live in the outer
halls, but was displaced last autumn
by Sajra and its crinwin minions.

357

Sites

2 		Story
						Sites should tell stories, even if
those stories are relatively simple. A site’s
story informs how you describe it and what
the PCs might find within.
If you’re prepping between sessions, then
give this process some time. Take notes.
Browse Book II. Let things percolate and
come back to them later.
If you’re doing this during play, keep it
short and simple. Go with the obvious
connections, questions, and answers.
Look for connections
Start with the site’s foundation (page
356), the details already established, and
the questions you’ve started asking yourself.
Consider how those things connect to:
		 Each other
		 Other setting elements in Book II
		 The setting’s timeline (see ages of the
world, Book II, page 496)
		 Things the players are interested in
		The PCs, their stuff, and their pasts
		 Established NPCs and threats
		 Previous events in-game
		 The questions on your “I wonder…”
list (page 33)
Let these connections prompt questions,
suggest answers, or just inspire new ideas.
Think about how all these elements fit
together in your site.

358

Ask yourself questions
Think about what you’ve got so far, and
start asking questions. Write them down!
Ask yourself about…
…		 the connections you’ve made and what
those connections imply. “What do the
Ustrina know about this ruin? Why do
they stay away?”
…		 any apparent contradictions. “If the site
is intact and fully visible, but close to
town, why has no one explored it?”
…		 fuzzy details. “It was a workshop, but
what type of work?” “Who was this
Forge Lord, specifically?”
…		 causes and reasons. “What melted the
statue?” “Why did they build this place
here, specifically?”
…		 how it’s changed over time. “How did
it fall into ruin?” “Who inhabited it
next? How did they change it?”
…		 the denizens who dwell here, their
motives, and how they interact with
each other. “What does the hagr want?”
“How do the bandits keep the ghosts
at bay?”
…		 timing. “How long have the crinwin
been here?” “How long was the barrow
sealed before Winoc opened it?”
…		 anything else that feels important.

Sites

Answer those questions
Start answering your questions, in whatever order makes sense or whatever order
the answers occur to you.
If there’s a clear answer to a question, then great, use that. Otherwise, jot
down some possible answers. Either pick
between them randomly or choose the
answer that excites you most, the one that
makes the most sense or feels the most
thematic. If unsure, put it aside for now
and come back to the question later.
If no answer occurs to you at all, or
you dislike the ones you’ve got, then flip
through your notes, your threats, and other
entries in Book II. Look for connections
that might help answer the question. Roll
on a relevant table in Book II, or use a
divination tool (e.g. tarot cards).
If you’re still stumped, consider whether
you need to answer this question, or if
it’s the right question. Add the question
to your “I wonder…” list (page 33) or
frame it differently. Maybe the question
isn’t, “How do the bandits keep the ghosts
at bay?” but rather, “Which of the bandits
have been possessed?”
Let your answers spawn new questions.
Answer them. If new connections occur to
you and prompt new ideas, consider using
them. Don’t be afraid to revise and adapt
your answers or even your foundation if
you need to.

Create a timeline
As the site’s story becomes clear to you,
start to create a timeline of key events. Put
them in a relative order: this happened,
then this, and then this. Vague times are
usually fine (centuries ago, decades ago,
years ago, last winter, last week, etc.).
If your site is more than a decade or so
old, refer to ages of the world (Book II,
page 496). Try to align your site’s timeline
with that of the setting.
If you’re prepping the site in advance,
then write this timeline down in as much
detail as you find useful. It’s typically a bullet list of key events, but you can include
details like the motives or experiences
of actors in the site’s story. If you’re so
inclined, you might even draw maps or illustrations of the place at various points in
time to visualize how it has changed.
If you’re improvising a site during play,
then keep things high-level. Focus on the
details relevant to play. If you’re creating a
Stone Lord ruin that’s been haunted by a
ghost for a few decades, then gloss over the
ruin’s origins and spend more time on the
ghost’s death and subsequent events.
New questions will arise as you make your
timeline—answer them! As you make new
connections, reconsider previous decisions.
Adjust your timeline accordingly.
When you think you’re done, read over the
timeline from beginning to end. Look for
obvious holes or contradictions. Address
them as needed.

359

Sites

I’ve laid the foundation for my Green Lord
tomb; it’s time to build up its story. I start
by looking for connections.

From there, I make a list of questions and then
start answering them.

The Green Lords bred the Fae to create false
afterlives for themselves. But when the Fae
rebelled, they “gutted tombs.” So why is this
tomb still intact? (A question for later!)

•		 Why didn’t the Fae gut the tomb during
their uprising? The inner vault is guarded
by powerful, loyal Fae. The outer tunnels
were looted and ransacked, but the vault
and Green Lord are secure.

The tomb is in the Great Wood, so the Forest
Folk must have known about it. The area is
probably marked with Forest Folk glyphs.

•		 When did Thornthumb move in? A few
centuries ago, after he lost his home in the
Fae revolt.

Rhianna and her pa found what looked
like a burnt offering left outside this place,
which they think the Forest Folk left.

•		 What did the Forest Folk think of this
place? They treated it as a sacred site,
petitioning the Green Lord like an oracle or
(fickle, dangerous) god.

Forest Folk descended from the Green Lord’s
mortal servants. There’s a mummified Green
Lord in there. Did they worship it?
Blodwen’s player is curious about the Forest
Folk; she might be able to learn something
about them here.
Sajra is a swyn. The Green Lords made
swyn with help from the Things Below, to
fight the Fae. The Fae hunt swyn.
Sajra moved in last autumn, and kicked
out a Fae named Thornthumb. Thornthumb
was a collector himself, so some of his hoard
must still be there.
Thornthumb would know all the ins and
outs of the tomb. He would have known
(and traded with?) the Forest Folk before
they left, too
Sajra has turned the crinwin into “fawning
servants,” but their instinct is “to covet.”
They’re probably jealous of the children’s
place in Sajra’s collection.
360

•		 What was Thornthumb’s relationship
with the Forest Folk? Did he act as an intermediary between them and the Green Lord?
Or charge them a toll for passing through his
home? That second one feels right.
•		 What caused Sajra to come here? Swyn
hate each other, but they do breed occasionally. I think Sajra is young and came here to
establish its territory? This makes me think
that swyn have genetic memory, and hatch
with much of their progenitors’ knowledge
and cunning.
•		 What minions did Sajra bring with it
when it arrived? If it’s young, probably not
many. Some crinwin from elsewhere, but
that’s it.
•		 What treasures did Thornthumb leave
behind? It could be anything. One of the
Mindgem’s missing parts? Eh, that just feels
too convenient. Not sure I need to answer
this just yet.

Sites

•		 How did Sajra get Thornthumb to leave?
(Threats? Violence? Trickery?) It took
over his mind and had him gift Sajra the
tomb and his hoard. Now he’s oathbound to
stay away.
•		 Has Sajra met the Green Lord? No, I
think it’s stymied by the guardian Fae. But
it wants access to the vault—it thinks it can
overthrow the Green Lord and claim its
treasure and servants.
•		 Where do the crinwin actually live? I
think Sajra loathes the crinwin. It lets the
most-trusted ones (who came here with it)
nest in the tomb, but not in its quarters. The
new crinwin nest outside.
•		 What’s the crinwin’s relationship to the
children? Crinwin are jealous but also
curious, creepily mimicking the kids’ words
and songs. The kids are scared of the crinwin, but mostly kept in a stupor.
•		 What do the crinwin think of Sajra? The
“trusted” ones basically worship it, and lord
over the new ones. They mimic Sajra’s voice
and collect its discarded “treasure.” The new
crinwin fawn over it and desperately want
to please it.
•		 What do they all eat? Crinwin forage
small game, grubs, roots, and (gross) food
for the kids. But Sajra goes out periodically
to hunt for itself. I can’t see it living on the
crinwin’s meager fare.
As I put the site’s timeline together, I realize
that the Fae rebellion happened in the Age of
the Makers, millennia ago, which means that
Thornthumb was there a loooong time. I bet he
used his sway over plants and trees to help keep
the place hidden.

When I’m done, the timeline looks like this:
IN THE TIME OF THE MAKERS
(millennia ago)
•		 Tomb is built, Green Lord interred
•		 Centuries later: Fae revolt starts!
Guardian Fae stay loyal, but rebels
ransack the tomb’s outer tunnels,
burn most mummified servants
•		 Thornthumb displaced by war; finds
tomb, moves in to outer tunnels, hides
the ruin with plants and growth
•		 War ends, tomb is largely forgotten
OVER THE NEXT FEW CENTURIES
•		 Forest Folk sometimes petition Green
Lord’s spirit for knowledge/aid
•		 Thornthumb collects tolls from Forest
Folk petitioners; his hoard grows
LAST TWENTY YEARS OR SO
•		 Rhianna & pa find burnt wolf here
•		 Forest Folk disappear; crinwin and
nailadd populations grow
•		 Sajra is hatched in Vor Svetelik
LAST AUTUMN
•		 Sajra arrives at tomb with crinwin
thralls in tow; makes Thornthumb,
promise to stay away from the tomb,
Sajra, its servants and possessions
•		 Sajra enthralls local crinwin; they
make nests outside the tomb
LAST WINTER INTO SPRING
•		 Crinwin kidnap Tiwlip from the Woods
•		 Crinwin raid Stonetop, kidnap Wynfor
and bring him back to the tomb
•		 Since thaw: crinwin have started
excavating other ruins near tomb
NOW
•		 Crinwin have kidnapped Pryder, are
dragging him towards the tomb.
361

Sites

3 		Contents
						Decide what the PCs might find
as they explore the site: its inhabitants, the
areas within it, the dangers and discoveries
that make it interesting, and the connective
tissue that holds it all together.
If you’re improvising the site during play,
you’ll do this spontaneously, inventing
the contents as needed based on the site’s
foundation and story, often picking from
ideas presented in Book II.
If you’re prepping between sessions, then
do the following steps in whatever order
makes sense. Take multiple passes. Start
high-level, with generalities. Then review
what you’ve got. Consolidate where you
can. Look for what’s missing and for interesting interactions. Revise. Make decisions
and create stuff as needed.
Populate it
Who or what lives or dwells here? Who or
what is visiting, exploring, or just passing
through? You probably already know this,
but make a list.
Don’t limit yourself to people. Think about
beasts, spirits, constructs, undead entities,
and any other entity that the PCs might
interact with.
If there are multiple denizens, then think
about the relationships between groups
and individuals. Factions and rivalries.
Debts, alliances, and feuds. Familial ties.
Love and hate and jealousy, all that stuff.
Include some notes about these relations as
you make your list.
If you expect the PCs to communicate
with any denizens, then create them as
NPCs (page 453), with tags, traits, an
instinct, roleplaying notes, etc.
362

Identify dangers & discoveries
Make a list of the dangers and discoveries
implied by the site’s foundation, story, and
occupants. This might include:
		Hazards (page 381) that the PCs
might need to deal with
		Monsters (page 391), i.e. denizens
that the PCs might fight
		Clues (page 424), especially those
that the PCs need to achieve their goals
		 Other, smaller sites (page 426)
		Non-hostile encounters (page 426)
with the site’s denizens
		Opportunities (page 428)
		Artifacts (page 428) and arcana
(page 438) found within the site
Refer to the relevant entries in Book II, or
make your own as needed.
The goal here is to identify the elements
that you want to include, and those that
you need to to account for. Don’t worry
just yet about placing them within the site,
or writing them up in detail. You can do
that now, if it helps you make decisions or
answer questions. But it’s often best to wait
until you’re doing the site’s final write-up
(page 368).

Sites

Establish areas/rooms
Write out the distinct rooms or places that
are suggested by your site's foundation
and story, by its denizens, and by common
sense. A small site might have just one
area, but most sites have at least a few.
If the site was built for a specific purpose,
start there. What rooms or areas would it
have had to fulfill that purpose? A home
would have a place to sleep, a hearth, some
sort of common room, a larder, etc. But a
fortress would have ramparts, a gate, a well,
maybe an inner keep.
Think about why the PCs are here in the
first place. If they’re looking for something,
what room or area is it in? Why is it there,
specifically? What does that tell you about
the site?
If you need to invent a new area or room
(to hold a danger or discovery, or to just
fill out the site), then create it as a new site
using the tools in Book II. If the PCs are
exploring a Stone Lord ruin and you need
to place a broken construct somewhere,
then roll up a new Stone Lord site (Book
II, page 384), treat it as an area within the
larger site, and place the construct there.

Describe the environment
Envision the site, or the individual area or
room you’re working on. Make some notes
as to how you’ll describe it.
For a larger site, think in generalities.
What sorts of rooms, areas, terrain, and
features can be found there? Build out a
vocabulary of places, imagery, impressions,
and details that you can pull from when
framing scenes and describing the site.
For smaller, discrete places, get specific.
What features of this place make it distinct?
Keep it brief for now, just a sentence or two,
enough to define the place in your head.
As you do this, think about the site’s story.
What traces remain of its original purpose?
What condition is it in now? How are the
current denizens using it, and how has
that changed or shaped the place? Reflect
the site’s story in how you describe the
environment.
If it helps you to draw maps or pictures at
this point, go for it!

363

Sites

I start sketching out the contents of my Green
Lord tomb with the following lists:
DENIZENS
•		 Sajra the swyn
•		 “Trusted” crinwin. Nests inside, came
here with Sajra and lord over the others. Collect Sajra’s castoff junk, mimic
its voice. Jealous of the kids.
•		 “New” crinwin. Nests outside, jealous
of everyone. Mimic kids’ songs, voices.
•		 Wynfor and Tiwlip. Entranced kids,
kept near Sajra. They sing for him!
•		 Pryder. Injured, imprisoned, out of it.
Not yet entranced?
•		 The mummified Green Lord, blissfully
unaware of the others
•		 The guardian Fae outside the vault
Other Fae/spirits in the Green Lord’s
false afterlife. Resentful? (Detail!)
DANGERS
•		 Swyn, Book II, p. 222
•		 Crinwin, Book II p. 61
•		 Green Lord Mummy, Book II p. 221
Magical wards on vault (create!)
Guardian Fae (create!)

Sites

I do a second pass to firm up the fuzzy bits. I
don’t bother with the Fae or spirits who dwell
in the Green Lord’s false afterlife (I’m not
sure that the PCs will even get in there),
nor with Sajra’s hoard or other treasures (In
a pinch, I can pick stuff from Book II).
For the inner vault, I think that the wards
involve a series of tests, meant to ensure that
petitioners approach the Green Lord with
proper respect. I write down:
•		 Halls of Humility & Gratitude (dreamlike tests)
•		 Hall of Offerings (guardian Fae, leads
to false afterlife)
I’m not sure what exactly these will entail;
I’ll come back to them later. I do spend time
creating the Almtakers (page 405), the Fae
who guard the Hall of Offerings. It seems
important to understand them, even if they
never appear in play.
Now, where is Sajra’s lair? It would be the
grandest place that remained in the outer
tomb. But what? I go back to the Green Lord
sites and roll up a new one to represent this
room. I get a theme of “spirits of the wild…
bound or put to use” and a purpose of “Esoteric (laboratory, shrine, surgery, etc.),” and
more stone slabs and carvings for architectural elements. I roll a theme from the spirits
of the wild (Book II, page 357): “water/
streams/ponds/lakes.”
Okay, I think this was a shrine (to Danu?),
featuring a natural spring and tended by
the spring’s spirit. The Fae and Thornthumb
left it intact, but Sajra has claimed it as its
den. Ooh, and Sajra’s presence is starting to
corrupt the place. Cool! (I add “Spirit of the
spring” to my list of denizens.)

If Thornthumb left the shrine intact, then
he probably dwelt in another chamber. I bet
it was, like, a memorial to the Green Lord,
a mosaic of moss depicting its achievements.
The Fae burned it during the revolt, but the
moss grew back under Thornthumb’s care (in
crazy-quilt patterns). The crinwin looted
the place under Sajra’s direction, but maybe
there’s still a hidden entrance that Thornthumb knows of ? Yeah!
Now, what’s going on with the children?
Wynfor (Caradoc’s nephew) and Tiwlip (the
girl who was taken first, last winter) are
enthralled by Sajra. I think it prefers their
company to the crinwin, so it keeps them in its
own chamber: clean (they wash in the spring)
and bedecked in ill-fitting silks (from Thornthumb’s hoard). They’re docile and dreamy,
but armed with bronze knives and they’ll
stab anyone who tries to take them away or
otherwise mess with Sajra’s stuff.
Pryder, meanwhile, arrived only recently.
He’s concussed and feverish, recovering in a
burial niche near Sajra’s lair. Sajra’s ordered
him to sleep, and I think the “new” crinwin
who brought him here are guarding him (to
the chagrin of the “trusted” crinwin).
For the “inside” criwin nests, my first idea is
to have them be in the entrance hall. But I
don’t really want them to be the first thing
the PCs encounter inside. I’d like to build up
some tension! The nests can be in a great hall
just outside the inner vault, big and grand,
now partially collapsed and strewn with
rubble. The crinwin nests are built on (old,
rotting) pillar-trees.

365

Sites

Arrange areas/rooms
Look at your rooms and areas and think
about how they’re arranged. Where else
can you get to from each area? What (if
anything) lies between them?

If you’re arranging your site concretely,
with specific connections between areas,
then think about the order in which the
PCs are most likely to explore them. How
will that shape the experience?

To represent the site’s layout, you might…

Linear connections (room A leads to room
B leads to room C) mean the PCs must
visit those areas in that order. Linear sites
also constrain how denizens react or where
they can go when the PCs approach.

…		 group them by region or zone;
…		 create a list of areas/rooms that you’ll
either pick from or roll on;
…		 write down the “exits” for each area or
room, and what each connects to;
…		 make a flowchart or nodal diagram;
…		 draw a representational map; or…
…		 use some variation or combination of
the above.
Which approach you use will depend on
personal preference, the complexity and
size of the site, and how “concrete” you
want the site to be.
As you arrange your site, you might realize
that something is missing, or decide to add
a new room or area as filler or connective
tissue. Or you might realize you have an
idea for a new danger or discovery. Or that
something you thought of earlier no longer
fits. So be it!

366

To make a site more dynamic, with more
decision points for the players and more
ways its denizens can respond, include:
		 Multiple paths through the site
		 Loops, where areas/rooms connect to
each other from multiple directions
		 Different levels and sub-levels
		 Multiple entry/exit points
		 Hidden, difficult, or dangerous paths
		 Paths that the site’s denizens can use
but the PCs can’t
Such additions make a site more interesting, but also more complicated to prep
and run. And multiple paths, levels, and so
on don’t always make sense. Sometimes a
simple, straightforward site is best—there
can be plenty of exciting moments and
meaningful decisions in a linear site!

Sites

I’ve been picturing the Green Lord’s tomb
as a series of halls and chambers encircling
the inner tomb, reflecting its dome-like
shape. I start by listing the areas and rooms,
like so:
OUTSIDE
•		 Clearing around hill
•		 Entrance to tomb (overgrown)
•		 Oaks atop hill (crinwin nests)
INSIDE
1. Entrance
2. Mossy chamber
•		 Hidden tunnel to outside
3. Great hall (crinwin nests)
•		 Entrance to inner tomb
4. Shrine (Sajra’s lair)
•		 Connects back to entrance
Between each of the above: halls with
(ransacked) burial niches

I’m thinking that the hall between the
shrine and the entrance will be mostly caved
in, with a narrow tunnel that Sajra and
the crinwin can squeeze through.
Upon reflection, that feels too linear. The
swyn and the crinwin are mobile, stealthy,
cautious foes and I want this to feel like a
game of cat-and-mouse. The layout should
support that.
I put the inner vault below the main level
of the tomb and add a central chamber between the entrance, mossy chamber, shrine,
and great hall. I think the central chamber
was a larger burial vault for the Green
Lord’s chief servants (ransacked, of course).
I draw the site out as a nodal diagram,
adding a few burrowed tunnels to the
outside—I don’t think Sajra or the crinwin
would be happy with only one exit!

Sites

4 		Write-up
						Once you’ve identified the site’s
contents, organize your notes and write
things up in as much detail as you find
useful, valuable, or fun.
The goal here is a document that helps
you run the site in play. And as you create
that document, you’ll end up honing your
craft. You’ll fill in gaps. You’ll find ways to
improve your environmental storytelling
and make the site more exciting. You’ll see
how the site fits together and fix things
that don’t make sense.
The nature of your write-up will vary from
site to site, and every GM’s approach is
different. One of your sites might need
a few paragraphs, a list or two, and some
references to Book II. Another site might
call for maps, room-by-room descriptions,
factions, tactics, and so on.
Remember: you’re writing this for yourself.
Don’t bother with flowery language unless
you plan to read it out loud. Your write-up
doesn’t need to be pretty or even complete;
it needs to be functional.
Create maps or visuals (maybe)
If you don’t already have one, consider
making (or using) a map or visual aid to
represent the site. This might be a nodal
diagram like the one on the previous page,
pictures, a representative map, or even 3D
terrain if that’s your jam.
Maps and visual aids help solidify the site
in your mind. They speed up play and make
it easier to describe the environment (“It
looks like this!”). They make it easier to
adjudicate PC actions and to decide what
the site’s denizens can perceive and how
they respond to the PCs.
368

But maps and visuals require a lot of work.
And maps in particular lock you into a
specific layout, making it harder to adapt
to pacing needs or brilliant ideas. If you
don’t have time, or if you prefer a looser
structure for this site, then don’t bother.
Detail each area/room
Give each area or room a title, something
you can refer to in your notes: “Entryway”
or “Long hall” or “room A,” etc.
Then write out the details and decisions
that you want to remember about that
area—the stuff you don’t want to forget or
think through in play. Include some or all
of the following:
		 A description of the environment
		 Impressions from various senses
		 Questions you might ask the PCs
		 Contents: NPCs, dangers, discoveries,
potential events (below)
		 Lore, backstory, or explanations
		 Exits and connections to other areas
For the contents, include whatever details
you need: numbers, where they’re found,
behaviors, tactics, etc. (see “Make plans”
on the next page). For content that has its
own stat block or write-up, include a page
reference, a copy of that write-up, or a link
as appropriate.
Subdivide as needed
If there are details that you find yourself
writing for multiple areas or rooms, pull
that out into its own entry.
Likewise, if certain dangers, discoveries, or
NPCs might be found throughout the site,
or are complex enough to warrant it, then
give them their own heading.

Sites

Find/create content
If you need to add something to an area or
the site as a whole, either pick something
from Book II (and tweak it as needed) or
create it from scratch. Refer to:
		Preparing hazards (page 384)
		Monsters (page 391)
		Prepping clues (page 425)
		Building encounters (page 426)
		Opportunities (page 428)
		Creating artifacts (page 434)
		Creating minor arcana (page 444)
		Creating major arcana (page 446)
		Creating NPCs (page 453)
		Writing moves (page 551)
If you realize you need to create a new
area, treat it like a smaller site. Lay its
foundation (page 356). Build up its story
(page 358). Sketch out its contents (page
362), and then write it up as needed.

Make plans
Think about how NPCs or monsters are
likely to react to the PCs, or what might
happen when the PCs enter an area, or
how long after X will Y happen. Reflect
this in your notes as…
…		 a descriptive sentence or two;
…		 one or more GM moves you can make;
…		 a series of if/then statements;
…		 a list of possibilities;
…		 a Die of Fate table;
…		 a custom player move;
…		 an impending doom and a series of
grim portents (page 290);
…		 a timeline or timetable of events; or…
…		 a combination of the above.
Review and revise
When you think you’re done, read through
your write-up. Is anything missing? Add it.
Is anything no longer needed? Cut it.

Make lists and tables
Some content exists in a quantum state.
The sentries walk a perimeter. The cave
bears might be out foraging. The ruin is
vast and you can’t be bothered to plot out
where each danger, discovery, or NPC is at
every moment.

Make sure it makes sense. What was the
site originally built or used for? Would it
have served that purpose? How is it being
used now, and does that actually work?
How do denizens come and go? How do
they see? Where do they sleep, store food
and prep it? What do they do with waste?

Put this sort of content into a list (or lists).
When the PCs enter a new area, or when
you want to make a GM move but aren’t
sure what to do, then pick something from
this list.

Do the site’s areas, layout, and contents reflect the answers to these questions? If not,
or if you can’t answer these questions at all,
then revise your write-up accordingly.

If you like, turn the list into a table. Assign
numbers 1-6 or 1-12 to the options and
have a player roll the Die of Fate to see
which option they get.
For examples, see the many regional and
site entries in Book II.

Give the write-up one last read-through,
looking for inconsistencies or conflicts. If
you find them, fix them.
It’ll never be perfect. There’s always something you’ll miss, something that could be
better. It’s okay. If anyone even notices, you
can fix it during play!
369

Sites

I’m ready to write up the Green Lord’s tomb!
I start by turning my nodal diagram into an
actual map of the main level, because it helps
me to picture the place.
From there, I start detailing the “Exterior,”
but quickly realize that I need to break it
up into the approach, the exterior of the site
proper, and the various entrances. I give the
crinwin their own write-up, because I want
to detail their numbers and tactics.
I move on to the interior, starting with the
entry chamber. I realize that I’m writing
things that are true about every room, so I
create an entry for “Interior (throughout).”
I write up the collapsed tunnel and its secret
burrow, then the central chamber. The central
chamber starts simple enough (vaulted ceiling, smashed sarcophagi, empty niches), but I
realize that this is where the rebel Fae would
have burned the bodies of the Green Lord’s
servants. I add details about the soot-stained
ceiling and scorched floor.
I write up the shrine next. I envision its
original state, then I imagine what Sajra has
done to it. He’s replaced the goddess’s statue
with his hoard, so I add “broken statue” to
the rubble in the central chamber. I write
up Tiwlip and Wynfor (the kids) and some
notes about how they live. Then I write up
the spirit of the spring (because Blodwen can
speak with spirits).
Next comes the mossy chamber. I create the
moss spirits as a bit of comic relief. Then I
realize that moss needs light and water, so
I add the glowing makerglass in the ceiling
and an irrigation channel in the floor. (I add
the channel to the map, the shrine, and the
central chamber, too.)
370

I write up the great hall, starting with the
pillars, the crinwin nests, and the stairs. I
want to include an “unstable construction”
hazard, telegraphing it with the collapsed
pillar, the rubble, and the sagging ceiling.
Then I prep the hazard as an impending
doom and grim portents.
I’m yet to decide where Pryder is. I know he’s
asleep in a burial niche, but where? I pick
randomly, placing him between the mossy
chamber and the great hall. I write up that
hallway, with the crinwin who kidnapped
Pryder jealously guarding him and some
“trusted” crinwin spying on them.
Speaking of crinwin: They get a second entry
for the interior. I consider making a “crinwin
encounter” table to reflect the weird stuff the
crinwin get up to, but the crinwin are almost
certainly going to be on alert when the PCs
enter the tomb. Instead of a table, I write up
some if/then tactics for them and a quick list
of things they might be up to if caught unawares. I also add some details to other areas
to reflect the crinwin’s relationship to wasps,
their obsessive hoarding, and their attempts
to ape Sajra.
Sajra also gets its own write-up. I realize
that it might not be in the tomb when the
PCs arrive, so I create a Die of Fate table for
that. I start writing some if/then tactics, but
Sajra is smart, mobile, stealthy, cautious
and magical—there are too many options! I
opt for a brief description of its priorities and
likely tactics.
I don’t bother with a map or detailed writeup of the inner tomb just yet. I picture it as a
fairly linear space largely separated from the
main floor. I doubt the PCs will head in there
this session. I’ll improvise if they do.

The Green Lord’s Tomb

As they approach

Hint at more than meets the eye: PCs
hear children singing (crinwin imitating
Tiwlip & Wynfor’s voices, but multiples
of the same voice).
		 Caradoc, you know this song; it’s one
you sang as a youth—what is it?
		 Vahid, how can you tell this is crinwin
mimicry, and not the missing kids?

Exterior

Wide, low hill, surrounded by trees. Three
oaks (still bare), last year’s grasses, some
briar, shrubs. Sparse saplings dot the hill.
		 Bits of worked stone poke out of the
hill’s soil and dead grass, easy to miss
		 Small crinwin nests in boughs of oaks
		 A faint odor: rotting cabbage, bad eggs
		 Faint hum of… bees? No. Wasps!

Present a discovery: Glyphs carved into
trees that surround hill, faint/worn, left
by the Forest Folk (see Book II, page 152);
mark this as a place of reverence, and refer
to the premnitsi (“the receivers”) and istina
(the “truth,” or… “oracle?”).

Sites

Crinwin (outside)
See Book II, page 61 for crinwin & wasps.
4 crinwin always on watch in the oaks or
nearby trees, often “singing” with the kids’
voices. 1d4+2 crinwin sleep in nests. 1d6-1
other crinwin are outside, doing crinwin
stuff (see page 376).
If sentries notice PCs: Stop singing, use
bird calls to alert others. Other crinwin
scatter, hide. Those in nests wake, stay
hidden.
If PCs poke around outside: Watch,
sneak closer. Keep signaling others via
birdsong.
If PCs try to enter: Sentries start howling
like wolves. Replies come from the Wood.
Crinwin inside go on alert. ~30 minutes
later, 1d6+9 Crinwin arrive back at tomb.
If PCs attack sentries: Flee into surrounding canopy (if possible) or into
tomb if not. If PCs persist in hounding
them, start wolf howls (as above).
If PCs molest a crinwin nest: Crinwin in
nest burst out, wasps swarm. Try to daze
or confound PCs, then flee.

Entrances
Main entrance: Recessed into the north
side of hill, at end of a ditch full of briar.
Briar obscures a 15 foot archway, partially
filled by soil and rubble. Leads to entrance
chamber (A). PCs can enter if they cut the
briar back or don’t mind getting scratched
(1d4 damage), but they risk agitating a nest
Secret tunnel: Opening is west of the hill,
30 feet past the tree line, under a boulder
and hidden by glamour and brush. A tight
squeeze for a PC with a light load. Leads
to the trapdoor [T] in the mossy chamber
(D). Unknown to Sajra/crinwin.
Burrows (S): Dug into the hill or rubble
about 18 inches across, visible only when
right next to them. Used by crinwin
and Sajra. Too small for PCs or Pryder.
Wynfor, Tiwlip, and Thornthumb could
theoretically squeeze through them.

Interior

White marble construction. Scrollwork
and bas relief of plants/chimerae, often
marred or busted. Dark unless otherwise
noted, but marble reflects/amplifies light.
		 Damp, cool air
		 Wooden archways, like they grew there
		 Crudely carved graffiti (vulgar images,
curses in Green Lord script)
		 Lazy, sluggish wasps
		 Distant echoes of voices/animal noises
Hallways are 10 feet wide and lined
with burial niches: 8 to 24 per hall,
human-sized but a few larger. No bodies,
but some have displays of junk (bones,
potsherds, rocks, maybe a bit of Value
1 finery with some flaw), set up by the
crinwin to mimic Sajra’s horde.

Wynfor & Tiwlip (small, entranced,

Pile of furs in northeast corner serve as a

Pryder’s hallway (F)
Pryder sleeps here in a burial niche. 1d4+2

Pryder (woods-wise, handsome, concussed):
HP 0 of 6; Damage d6 if armed; Instinct
to see the good in things. Notes: Asleep
per Sajra’s command. Head wound, cuts/

mind is free.

Crinwin nests hug the pillars up near the

Three 20-foot-tall pillars (trees, basically).

Wasps: Colonizing crinwin nests, pillars.

Sites

Crinwin
See Book II, page 58. ~30 crinwin total,
about half are “trusted” (replace cautious
with zealous). “New” crinwin flee if half
their numbers are lost; “trusted” crinwin
fight to death.
4 sentries outside. 4-10 total watching
Pryder in hallway (F). 3-6 asleep outside.
3-6 sleeping in great hall (G). 10-15 off
foraging or away on tasks. Up to 5 others
doing crinwin stuff (see below).
If alerted to intruders: 2-3 keep tabs
while others rally near entrance hall (A).
Once ~10 have gathered (10-20 minutes),
mimic Tiwlip/Wynfor calling for help to
lure PCs to entrance, then swarm them.
If Sajra, its hoard, or kids are threatened:
Nearby crinwin raise the alarm and attack.
If caught unawares, will be doing crinwin stuff: eating/processing food, expanding or repairing nests, fighting over junk,
bullying each other, mimicking Sajra or
the kids, talking/singing in their voices.
