has advantage.

562

6 		Write a full draft

						Write out the move, beginning to
end, keeping in mind everything you’ve
done so far. Don’t worry about polished
language or formatting yet, unless that
helps you write. Your goal here is to see
how it all fits together.
As you write, you might rethink decisions
you made earlier. Something might not
work as well as you thought it would, or
you might think of a better way. Great.
Change it. Creativity is often messy.
Keep your goals and constraints in mind.
Let them guide your decisions, your
choices, your words.
If your move involves someone picking
between results, you can either write the
choices inline, or you can make a pick list.
An inline choice looks like this: “on a 7-9,
you either succeed OR no one’s the wiser
(your choice)”. This approach saves space,
so it’s useful when brevity is a concern. But
it’s often less clear than a pick list.
Use a bulleted pick list any time there are
3 or more options to pick from, unless you
have a very good reason to do otherwise.
Even with 2 options, a pick list can often
be clearer and easier for players to process.
With that said, keep your pick lists short.
2-4 is ideal. 5 or 6 can work, but see if you
can condense them. 7 or more options in a
single move is usually a bad idea, as it can
lead to decision paralysis.

Writing Moves & Love Letters

Try to make the different choices parallel,
in their language and their scope, scale,
timeframe. Don’t make one of the choices
obviously and always better than the rest.
If you have a pick list, consider whether
any of your options would benefit from an
“else” statement at the end. For example,
one option for Forage (page 336) is “you
avoid danger or risk (else, there is some).”
The ”else” there clarifies that if they don’t
pick this option, there will be danger or
risk. You can also use “else” statements
to establish a baseline result in a succinct
way, which can be quite useful for magical
effects. See Ice Weaving and Impudent
Polymorph for examples (Book II, pages
515 and 525).
Okay, here’s my full draft:
When you take shrooms together near
a waystone, under a full moon, roll
+WIS. If you’ve taken shrooms before,
roll with advantage. On a 10+, you
experience a profound sense of connectedness to your companions and
the world; on a 7-9, pick 1:
•		 Mark a debility
•		 It’s a bad trip, and you’re kind of
freaking out
•		 You’re not really feeling it—you
need to take another dose and roll
again at disadvantage
On a 6-, pick 2 from the list above and
you draw unwanted attention from
the spirit world.
Regardless, you’re hallucinating now,
and your eyes are open to the spirit
world and the Fae paths. Have fun!

Hold and spend
Some moves have the player “hold
x currency,” where the currency is
something specific like “Readiness”
or “Nerve.” The move then says how
the player can spend that currency,
and what happens when they do. That
could be a mechanical bonus (as with
Bolster, page 524), something happening in the fiction (like with Silver
Tongued, page 111), or some combo
of the two (see Defend, page 216).
Hold-and-spend is a good choice for
moves that…
…		 reflect a PC’s preparations, like A
Safe Place (page 130) does;
…		 provide a limited, ongoing bonus,
like Anger Is a Gift (page 138);
…		 need to limit how often a PC can
use them, such as Stentorian,
page 127); and/or…
…		 reward behaviors, like Chronicler
of Stonetop (page 118) does.
Your move might grant a set amount
of currency each time it’s triggered
(see Piety, page 123), or a variable
amount based on a roll (see Silver
Tongued, page 111), fictional details
(see Prepare a Welcome, page 126),
or both (see Defend, page 216).
Name the currency something
unique, or at least something that
isn’t being used in your game. Include
a place to track currency on whatever
handout you give the players, usually
with a series of s. Consider whether
you need to spell out when the player
loses all held currency, or if that’s
obvious from context.
563

Writing Moves & Love Letters

7 		Review and revise

						Read your drafted move. Revise it.
Make it better!
Imagine it in play
Envision a situation where this move
could come up in play. Picture your players
triggering it. Run through the move. Try
to read it with fresh eyes. Imagine your
players’ reactions. If anything strikes you as
unclear, confusing, or awkward, then fix it.
Run through the possible outcomes. Do
they all make sense in this scenario? If you
can’t envision what a given result (or combination of results) would look like, then
something’s wrong. Rethink the result(s),
or how it prompts you to pick them.
Repeat this process a few times, imagining
a different potential situation each time.
Make sure it meets your goals
Think back on what you intended this
move to do (see page 552). Is it doing
that? Is it doing it better than what you’d
get if you just relied on the core loop, your
GM moves, and the player moves already
in play? If not, why not? How can it better
meet your goals?
Consider your constraints for this move.
Does it fit in the space you have? Does it
account for the needs and preferences of
your group? If not, revise until it does.
Check for common pitfalls
Is the trigger too broad or too specific,
such that the move will trigger more often
or less often than you want? If so, fix it.
Does your trigger confound outcome with
intent? Have you written “When you solve
the puzzle” instead of “When you try to
solve the puzzle”? If so, clarify the trigger.
564

Does your move apply player mechanics to
monsters/NPCs? For example, does it say,
“Your foe has disadvantage on their next
move” or “They mark dazed.” Monsters and
NPCs don’t use those rules! Rewrite the
result to impose a fictional limitation, or to
grant PCs an opportunity or bonus against
it. (It is okay to modify the damage rolls of
monsters or NPCs, or to apply modifiers
or debilities to followers.)
Does your move refer to “rounds,” “turns,”
or other concepts that don’t exist in Stonetop? Use fictional equivalents instead (like
“for a few moments,” or “until they can
clear their head”).
Are any of the results boring? Do they
amount to “nothing happens,” or “try
again?” If so, try to restructure the move so
that there’s a meaningful result or change
every time it triggers.
Is the move purely mechanical? Sometimes
this makes sense, like with Improved
Stat (page 106). But most moves should
prompt interesting fiction via their triggers
and/or generate it via their results.
If the move involves a pick list, are all the
options equally appealing (or equally bad)?
For each option, can you imagine a time
when a player would pick this over one of
the others? If not, either make the option
more appealing or cut it entirely.
Does the move blatantly cross the Line
(page 195) of GM/player authority? Does
it have the player decide what NPCs or
other PCs do, or invent new details about
the current situation that their PC would
have no influence over or prior knowledge
of ? If so, change what decisions get made,
or who makes them.

Writing Moves & Love Letters

Is this a playbook or arcanum move, or
some other move meant to give the PC an
edge? If so, make sure it’s actually letting
them do something they couldn’t do without the move, or letting them do it better.
If it’s just a more-specific version of Defy
Danger, then what’s the point?
Is the move overly complex? Does it have
too many variations or variables? Does it
have a lot of steps or dependencies? If so,
simplify it. Rein in its scope if you need to.
Cut any decision that isn’t crucial. Reorder
the procedure so that it flows naturally and
intuitively. If need be, split this out into
multiple moves.
Is the move written defensively? Does it
use legalistic language to prevent abuse or
loopholes? If so, does it need to? Can you
rely on context and the players’ agenda
(page 37) and principles (page 44)
to prevent such abuse? You do sometimes
need to clarify intent and establish what a
move can’t do, but keep it short and sweet.
Punch it up
Look for ways to make the move grabbier,
more exciting, more thematic. Formidable
(page 114) doesn’t trigger “when a battle
starts.” It triggers “When you wade into
battle.” When the Lightbearer gets a 10+
on Guiding Light (page 122), “you all
make it through (Helior be praised).” That
little prayer at the end adds a lot!

Edit for length and clarity
Cut any word that’s unneeded. Read each
sentence, look for a cleaner way to phrase
it. Reduce the move to its simplest version,
while maintaining clarity and impact.
Check grammar, formatting
Make sure you’re using the right words,
that your subjects and verbs agree, that
your bullet lists use parallel language.
Check your spelling. If typing them out,
put triggers in bold and italic text. Put
thresholds in bold, like, “on a 10+:…”
Grammar and formatting are less crucial
for a move you’ll use just once at your own
table. But the more you plan to use it or
share it, the more important this becomes.
Get feedback
Ask others for feedback. Share your move
online, with other Stonetop players and
GMs. Share it with your players, too (unless you want it to be a surprise).
Call out specific areas where you’d like
input, but be open to any feedback you get.
You need not take the advice of strangers on the internet, but at least consider
whether they have a point.
Remember: you can revise your move once
you see it in play. Even a move that you use
only once can be clarified in play. Your move
doesn’t need to be perfect before you use it.
If you’re creating something to publish or
share with other groups—like a playbook,
arcanum, or supplement—then seek
feedback widely and playtest the moves as
much as you can. Adjust them based on
how they actually perform in play.

565

Writing Moves & Love Letters

I frame a scene in my mind: the Great
Wood at night, the party encamped near a
waystone. They’ve been here for hours, waiting for moonrise. It’s time! They get a little
solemn as Blodwen passes out the shrooms.
They trigger my move!
I haven’t really accounted for Rhianna’s
crew, but do I need to? Order Followers
will have Rhianna roll the move for them.
They’re a group, so they’ll roll just once and
we’ll apply the results to specific individuals.
That works, I’m good.
I imagine we’ll go around asking who’s
taken shrooms before, to see who rolls with
advantage. That’ll be fun.
The players will each roll +WIS. Someone
(let’s say Blodwen) would get a 10+. “ You
experience a profound sense of connectedness
to your companions and the world.” Which
is… boring, actually! Reviewing my goals,
I planned for this move to explore the PCs’
emotions and relationships. Without it, I’d
use Keep Company to do just that. So, I
revise the 10+ result to have them ask each
other (and the world) a meaningful question.
I imagine Rhianna rolling a 7-9. Hmm.
She’d probably pick “It’s a bad trip, and
you’re kind of freaking out,” which I realize
overlaps with marking dazed. And I could
use that “bad trip” choice to explore some
darker emotions.
Let’s say Caradoc gets a 6-. He’d draw
unwanted attention from the spirit realm,
mark a debility, and have a bad trip. So:
he’d see the shadowy presence in his mind’s
eye, it would see him back, and he’d be left
shaken by it. Cool!

566

Then I imagine Vahid getting a 6- and
picking “bad trip” and “not really feeling it.”
He’d maybe draw the ire of some spiteful
nature spirits, and freak out, and… need a
bigger dose? That doesn’t feel right. And it’d
lead to just rerolling, and maybe picking the
same thing. No, the “not feeling it” option
has to go.
I think a bit and rewrite the move. As I do,
I simplify the trigger—it didn’t need to be
so specific after all! I also run it by a player
and incorporate some of their feedback. I
end up with this:
When you take shrooms in the Great
Wood, roll +WIS. If you’ve taken them
before, you have advantage. On a 10+,
ask one of these to one other player,
and one to the GM who will answer for
the world at large:
•		 What do you love most about me?
•		 What saddens you about me?
•		 What is your dearest hope for me?
On a 7-9, pick 1; on a 6-, all 3:
•		 Mark a debility
•		 Guilt and shame boil up within you;
describe the vision/memory that
comes with them
•		 You draw unwanted attention from
the spirit world
Regardless, you’re hallucinating now,
and your eyes are open to the spirit
world and the Fae paths. Have fun!
I run through all the combinations again
and they all feel good. I consider posting it
online for feedback, but the game starts in
an hour. This is good enough!

Writing Moves & Love Letters

More examples

Here’s another example of a situational
move, this one written to reflect the party’s
current, sour mood. (That last bullet was
originally “What do you do that I find so
annoying?” But a fellow GM suggested
this replacement. Hooray for feedback!)
MISERY LOVES COMPANY
When you Keep Company while discord plagues the party, take turns
asking the following questions instead:
•		 What duty are you/we neglecting?
•		 What’s eating at you, and how do
you take it out on others?
•		 How do I make things worse?
•		 What little thing do we find
ourselves bickering about?
•		 What old crap do you just have to
bring back up?
Here's an example of a hazard move, a
ward that scares intruders off with their
own fear. It’s meant to serve as a legitimate
barrier, but also to bring the PCs’ fears to
the forefront as they enter this haunted
barrow mound.
When you approach the threshold
with the intent to cross, it fills you
with dread. If you persist, roll +WIS:
on a 10+, describe a deep fear from
your past and how you overcame it,
then cross; on a 7-9, mark a debility
and tell us of an unconquered fear
that lurks in your heart, then cross if
you wish; on a 6-, mark a debility and
describe the fear that overcomes
you—you will not cross willingly while
the ward persists.

Here's a new expedition move, written for
specific players in a specific game. It’s a riff
on Burgle (page 110), and was prompted
by players hogging the spotlight with
solo missions. They’d push their luck until
disaster struck, and play was grinding to a
halt. So: new move, everybody!
SCOUT AHEAD
When you go off on your own into a
dangerous place, the GM can have
you Scout Ahead. If they do, say how
you do it and roll +STAT (per Defy
Danger). On a 7+, you make it back
and the GM says where you got to
and what you learned. Then, on a
10+: also pick 2; on a 7-9, also pick 1:
•		 You got away clean, rousing no
suspicion or alarm
•		 You set something up to exploit upon
your return
•		 Ask a Seek Insight question about
what you saw
On a 6-, you either make it back
to the others with trouble in tow, or
you’re missing in action (your call).
Of course, if any PC can Scout Ahead
then Burgle is worthless. If there was a
Fox in the group, I’d also change Burgle to
something like this:
BURGLE
When you Scout Ahead, you can pick
1 extra option (even on a 6-, pick 1),
and add this to your list of options:
•		 You swiped something useful and/or
valuable

567

Writing Moves & Love Letters

Writing
love letters

Love letters are like single-use custom
moves, addressed to a specific PC. Write
them as part of your prep and hand them
out at the start of a session (or whenever it
makes sense). They’re especially useful for:
		Glossing over downtime
		Resolving individual PC projects, or
giving them a chance to make progress
		Filling in periods where a PC is “off
camera,” like if they take a solo trip or
their player misses a session
		Wrapping up a situation that the group
doesn’t want to play out in detail
		Starting a session with momentum,
hard-framing a scene or situation and
jumping to the “good stuff ”
		Digging into a PC’s backstory, relationships, or inner thoughts
		Giving players setting info without it
being a one-sided lore dump (see page
266 for an example)
You might write a love letter to just one
PC (like when their player missed the last
session). But often, you’ll write each PC a
love letter and run through them in turn.
Love letters are an investment. They take
time to write, and skill to write well. They
get used only once. But they’re powerful
tools, and writing them is great practice for
writing moves in general.

To write a love letter, consider first and
foremost what you’re trying to establish:
what you need the letter to declare as true.
Given that, ask yourself:
		 What elements do you want to hand
over to the player to define?
		 What could go well for the PC?
		 What could go poorly?
As far as structure goes:
		Love letters don’t have triggers; start
them with “Dear <PC>”
		Follow that with as much context as
you need (though keep it tight)
		Give the player specific prompts for
the details you want them to add, as
part of either the setup or the results
		Decide how you’ll pick between results
(page 558), and if there’s a roll, what
that roll looks like (page 560)
Pick a structure that seems to fit your
needs, and start writing. Don’t force it—if
the structure isn’t working, try something
else. Get a working draft, then tweak the
language and details as time and interest
permit. Don’t stress over it too much. A
love letter gets used only once, and if it
doesn’t land, you can clarify it in play.
Print the love letter out or write it on an
index card, or send it to the player online.
During play, have them read the letter
aloud and do what it says. If you write love
letters for multiple PCs, you can set them
up to be read in a specific order, or let the
players choose who goes when.
Take notes! Incorporate the results into
this and future sessions. Make the results
and their choices matter.

568

Writing Moves & Love Letters

Some examples:

Dear Vahid,

Dear Rhianna,
You got back from a few days hunting to find everyone talking about
Caradoc, Vahid, and their trip to the
Tower. As if you didn’t have enough
to worry about. Speaking of worries,
roll +Fortunes: on a 10+, pick 1; on a
7-9, pick 2; on a 6-, mark XP and all 3.
•		 Owain’s been calling for folks to
“do something” about Vahid—who
has been agreeing with him, that
you would not have expected?
•		 Wini (Cerys’s eldest) has been acting
out, badly—how so?
•		 Crinwin jumped one of your crew
while out hunting (who?); they’re
home, but badly hurt (how so?)
XOXO – your GM

Dear Caradoc,
You and Morwenna are still tied up,
but you’re at the greenhouse. Tell
us what unpleasant truth you had
to reveal to seal the deal, then roll
+CHA: on a 10+, pick 2; on a 7-9, pick
1; on a 6-, pick 1, mark XP, and your
captors clearly know they’re being
followed—they’re laying a trap!

It’s been a quiet, cozy winter, with
plenty of time inside. What unlikely
aspect of a personality has the Mindgem revealed, now that you’ve spent
so much time with it?
Now, ask the Mindgem a question
about the Makers, their history, or
their arts. You’ve gleaned a clear
and useful answer, and even a few
follow-up questions. But also, roll +WIS:
on a 10+, you’ve not neglected anyone nor anything; on a 7-9, tell us who
you’ve let down in some little way; on
a 6-, mark XP, and tell us who you’ve
let down and why they’re still upset.
XOXO – your GM

Dear Blodwen (read after Rhianna),
Dawn finds you all hiding in a cart,
rattling out into the fields after a
sleepless night tending to Tierny and
the others (with Brin’s able help).
What personal advice did you give
Brin before you left?

•		 You’ve slyly loosened your bonds
•		 Morwenna’s calm & playing along
(else, she’s freaking right out)
•		 They haven’t separated you and
Morwenna yet (else, they have)

As for Tierny, roll +INT. If Rhianna’s
turn led to casualties, then roll with
disadvantage. On a 10+, Tierny has
made a full recovery and few will
know how close she was to death; on
a 7-9, she’s up and will recover, but
your name will spread as a miracleworker; on a 6-, either she dies and
becomes a martyr, or lives on as a
husk of her former self (your call).

XOXO – your GM

XOXO – your GM

The Game Ongoing

Ending a session
Between sessions
Starting a session
Pacing
Advancement
Retiring PCs
New PCs
Conflict between PCs
Time skips
Shake-ups
Ending your game

571

The Game Ongoing

Stonetop is built for long-term play. You can
get a satisfying experience in as few as 3-5
sessions, but it’s best when you and your
group commit to an ongoing game.
In this chapter, we’ll discuss:

572

		Managing the game across sessions
		How the game itself changes over time
		Issues you might encounter on the way
		Bringing your game to a satisfying end
This chapter contains no rules, no new
mechanics (save one). It’s a collection of
advice, tools, and practices to help you and
your friends get the most out of your game.

The Game Ongoing

Ending
a session
Do the move

END OF SESSION
When a session ends, point out how
you demonstrated or struggled with
your instinct. If you can, mark XP.

Make time for the End of Session move
(at right) each time you play. Start to wrap
up the action 30 minutes before folks need
to leave, and give yourselves at least 15
minutes to do the End of Session move.
Set a timer. It’s important.

Say how your relationship with or
opinion of a PC, NPC, or group has
changed. If you can, mark XP.

Take notes! Not just the wishes, but all of
it: the times where each PC engaged with
their instinct, the changes to relationships,
what the group learned and accomplished,
and what each player praised. These notes
are invaluable between sessions, as you
reflect and try to recall what happened.

		 Did we learn more about the
			 world or its history?
		 Did we defeat a threat to
			 Stonetop or the region?
		 Did we improve our standing
			with our neighbors?
		 Did we make a lasting improve			 ment to Stonetop, or tangible
			 progress towards doing so?

The last two steps of the move—praise
and wishes—are the most important. They
allow you to calibrate the game and create
space for everyone to give feedback.
If the move reveals a larger issue—like
a conflict between players or dissatisfaction with the game—then do a
check-in (page 580). If it involves
the whole group and you’ve all got
time and space, then do it now.
If that’s not practical, or just
one or two players are
involved, then take it as
a follow-up to discuss
the issue later.

Answer these questions as a group.
For each “yes,” everyone marks XP.

Praise something about the session
(in the fiction or around the table)
that you enjoyed or appreciated.
Finally, offer up a wish for future
sessions: more __, less __, a chance to
__, handling __ in a different way, etc.
Wishes can be about what happens
in the fiction or around the table. The
GM will take notes.
For more guidance, see page 232 of the
Player Moves chapter.

573

The Game Ongoing

Handle logistics

Schedule the next session if you can. If
not, pick a tentative date or two and make
plans to confirm. Games die more often
from scheduling neglect than from lack of
interest.
Identify follow-up tasks. Who needs to do
what between now and next time? Assign
responsibilities and when they need to be
done by. For example:
		 All: Confirm availability (tomorrow)
		 Hobbes: Pick date for next session,
send invites (following day)
		 Jamie: Level Up (by next session)
		 Jeremy: Make a Plan for Vahid’s
gizmo (by next session)
		 All: Post goals for winter (by Friday)
Ask one of your players to track these tasks
and follow up, ensuring that they get done.
You’ve got enough to juggle. Let someone
else do the badgering.
Finally, help the host clean up. It’s basic
courtesy, and shows respect for the space
and the people you’re sharing it with.

574

Take notes

What happened this session that you don’t
want to forget? What are your next steps?
Write that stuff down. Now. While it’s still
fresh in your mind.
You don’t need to write a full session recap
right now (it’s late, you’re probably tired).
But try to capture:
		Your personal follow-up tasks (“Make
a Plan for Vahid’s gizmo,” or “Talk to
Bob about kibitzing”)
		NPCs you introduced and their key
details (“Stamat: sadistic mercenary;
flamboyant; scarred, dead eye”)
		Important decisions you made on the
fly, especially those not yet revealed in
play (“Callum is dead” or “Nanzl knows
Brynmor is lying”)
		Consequential events that you want to
make relevant in future sessions
(“Nanzl got a lock of Kios’s hair,” or
“Sawyl is pissed at Nolwen”).
		What you had in mind when you
dropped a hint or introduced some
provocative detail (“Silver goo is sign of
the corrupted hagr,” or “Una’s fiery gem
= heart of the Mighty Servant”)
		Decisions you know you need to make
(“What is in Piotr’s cellar?”)
		Prep you know you’ll want to do
(“Draw a map of the barrow”)

The Game Ongoing

Between
sessions

Session recap(s)

Reflection

If one of your players takes session notes,
then ask them to post those notes somewhere public. Review them before you start
prepping for the next session, and make
corrections or additions as needed.

Spend time after each session reflecting on
the previous one. Ask yourself:

You might find it helpful (or necessary) to
make a session recap yourself. Maybe your
players don’t take notes, or their notes are
so detailed that the other players will never
read them. Maybe they leave out stuff that
you think is important.

Use the time between sessions to tend to
the game: to reflect, take notes, prep, and
make plans.
Remember: GMing is a practice (page
203). You get better at it by doing it, and
by trying to do better next time.

		 What goals had you set for yourself ?
Did you meet them? If not, why not?
		 What went well? What are you proud
of ? What did your players praise?
		 What did your players wish for? How
can you make those wishes come true?
		 Where did the game falter or drag?
Where did you feel uncertain? What
would you do differently, in retrospect?
		 Did you pursue your agenda (page
168)? Did you follow your principles
(page 192)? If not, how was the game
impacted? What would it have looked
like if you had?
You might do this formally, sitting down
and taking notes. Or maybe you just think
about it while you commute or do chores.
However you go about it, reflect on the
session. Don’t beat yourself up, but be honest. Consider what worked and what didn’t.
Think about why, and about how you can
do better.
Write down 1-3 goals for the next session.
As you prep, keep these goals in mind.
During the session, put them somewhere
you can see them. Try to meet them.

Every session should get a written recap,
an official history of what happened. Few
people can recall the events of a session
a few days later, much less after weeks or
months. So make sure the key moments of
each session get written down.

There’s no one right way to do this. Some
groups like a detailed, scene-by-scene
breakdown. Others prefer a bullet-point of
key events, like this:
Caradoc and Vahid:
•			 Went to Ruined Tower
•			 Ghost stories at the Crossroads!
•			 Explored outer ruins, got makerglass
shard and bronze plaque w/spell
•			 Beznpol attacked; took its hide
Blodwen:
•			 Big public fight with Tegwen, renounces
her as kin
•			 Moves out, moves in with Seren
•			 Tegwen is petty & mean (surprise!)
•			 Lewela visits, tearful support
Rhianna (not present, off hunting?)
Do whatever works for your table, but
make sure a running record of play exists,
and that it’s available to all the players.
575

The Game Ongoing

Bookkeeping

Pull out your notes, the steading playbook,
your threat tracker—whatever tools you’re
using—and bring them up to date.
1)		 Update your threats, following the
procedure on page 298.
2)		 Update your NPCs, wherever you
track them. Cross off those who’ve
died, update notes for existing ones,
and add new NPCs from the previous
session. See Creating NPCs (page
453) for guidance.
3)		 Update followers. If you expect an
NPC to start acting as a follower, then
write them up using the steps on page
474. Then review and update any
existing followers, per page 480.
4)		 Update the steading playbook (page
154), to reflect changes to Fortunes,
Surplus, debilities, resources, fortifications, and assets. Mark progress made
towards improvements.
5) Review ﻿content guidelines (page 66)
and update them if needed, based on
player discussions and your own needs.
If you realize that the guidelines need
to change, or that your group has been
deviating from them, check in with the
players (page 580) or make a note to
do so.

6)		 Review any loot the PCs acquired.
Make sure it’s on someone’s inventory
insert or otherwise accounted for. If
you need to provide players handouts
or write-ups, make a note to do so. If
you haven’t revealed what the loot is
or does, consider how you will—see
“Identifying artifacts” (page 430)
and “Identifying arcana” (page 440).
7)		 Update your “I wonder…” list (page
33). If you’ve answered any of these
questions, then jot the answer down
and cross off the question. Have any of
the questions changed, evolved, gained
clarity or nuance? Update them. Have
any new questions occurred to you?
Add them to the list.

Follow-ups

As the session ended, you likely found
yourself with some follow-up tasks. As you
reflect and take care of bookkeeping, you’ll
likely identify more. Review your to-do list
and prioritize:
		Start with time-sensitive tasks, stuff
that needs to happen before the next
session (like confirming availability,
proposing a big retcon, or reviewing a
player’s special request).
		Next, focus on tasks relevant to the upcoming session (like making a key
decision about a threat, or writing up
an artifact that the PCs acquired).
		For everything else, work on them only
if you feel inspired to do so and you
know you have time. Otherwise, keep
them on your to-do list until they
become timely. Don’t Make a Plan now
for a player whose PC will be busy for
the next few sessions.

576

The Game Ongoing

Prep

As the next session approaches, make time
to sit down and prep. Think about where
things left off last session. Where are the
PCs and what are they up to? What do
you expect to happen in the next session?
Prep for that.
If the PCs are…
…		 embarking on a journey, then see the
What to prep section of the Expeditions chapter (page 340).
…		 already on a journey, review your prep.
Where are the PCs? What are the next
legs of travel, points of interest, and
challenges? Which encounters are most
likely to come up in the next session?
Flesh them out if you need to.
…		 about to explore a site, then prep it
to whatever level of detail you find
useful and have time for. See Creating
sites on page 355.
…		 exploring a site already, what are
they likely to encounter next? Flesh
that out if you need to.
…		 likely to face any dangers or make any
discoveries, then prep those if you
have not already. Find stuff from Book
II and review those entries, or make
your own. For guidance, see:
		Preparing hazards (page 384)
		Monsters (page 391)
		Clues (page 424)
		Encounters (page 426)
		Opportunities (page 428)
		Artifacts (page 434)
		Minor arcana (page 444)
		Major arcana (page 446)

…		 likely to interact with NPCs, review
your notes on them. If you think you’ll
introduce new NPCs, see Creating
NPCs (page 453) for guidance.
…		 likely to gain a new follower, then find
their stats in Book II or make them
from scratch (page 474).
…		 heading home or into a stretch of
downtime, then prep for a homefront
session. See “What to prep” on page
544 of the Homefront chapter.
Other things you might prep
		An adventure hook to kick off a new
adventure (page 578)
		Custom moves (page 551) to use in a
specific situation.
		Love letters (page 568) to use at the
start of a session.
		Setup questions (page 262) like
those you asked at the start of the First
Adventure, as a way to warm players
up, establish details, tie back to prior
events, or foreshadow future ones.
		Start of session moves. For example,
if you have a Would-be Hero with the
Destined background (page 137),
prep a vision or omen for them!
		Specific scenes you’d like to play out:
their purpose, descriptions, lines of
NPC dialogue, GM moves, etc.
		Play aids like maps, visuals, player
handouts like arcana cards, etc.
		Stuff to support player wishes and
your personal goals (page 578).

577

The Game Ongoing

Adventure hooks
If the last session ended in downtime, you
might want to kick off a new adventure.
Maybe the Seasons Changed and you
owe the players an opportunity or threat.
Maybe they got a 7-9 to Pull Together
and choose “unforeseen cost, requirement,
or challenge.” Maybe the Ranger tried to
Persuade Gorlas to leave that haunted ruin
alone, but rolled a 4, and so old Gorlas—
he ain’t gonna listen.

Wishes and personal goals
Look over the wishes that your players
gave you at the end of the last session, or
any unmet wishes from before that.

To prep for a new adventure, follow the
process laid out in the First Adventure
chapter (page 251). Or, if it makes sense,
prep a full-on crisis (page 499) instead.

For each wish or goal, what would help
you achieve it? Work that into your prep!
This could mean:

578

Look over the goals you wrote down as
part of your reflection (page 575), or
write 1-3 goals for yourself now—things
you’d like to work on, do better, or just remember to do. It’s fine (ideal, even) if these
overlap with your players’ wishes.

		Prepping a specific hook, site, danger,
etc., or prepping it in a certain way
		Making a list, reminder, or cheat sheet
and keeping it somewhere visible
		Writing a to-do list for the session,
with scenes to frame, GM moves to
make, questions to ask, etc.
		Writing a custom move (page 551)
to handle a specific situation
		Brainstorming possible impressions,
GM moves, bits of NPC dialogue,
ways to resolve player moves, etc.

The Game Ongoing

During the End of Session, Vahid’s wish
is for a lead on completing the Mighty
Servant. So between sessions, I prep a
hook: a traveler selling an old scroll that
describes a fiery glass “heart,” powering a
device in the fallen city of Tierstered.
One of my goals is to use multiple senses
when describing environments. So, I
write “SOUND • SMELL • FEEL”
across the top of my GM playbook.
During some homefront sessions, I’ve
struggled to engage Blodwen’s instinct
of Preservation (“to protect the natural
world”). So, I add this to my prep:
		 Play them against each other:
Glennys objects to Blodwen about
Rhianna’s plans for palisade.

Don’t over-prep!
Remember why you prep: to give yourself
cool stuff to say, to speed up play, to shore
up weaknesses, to hone your craft, and to
give yourself permission to play hard and
inflict real consequences. With that in
mind, prep only:
1)		 What you need to prep, like stats for a
monster the PCs will fight, or a love
letter to a player who missed a session
2)		 What you can’t easily improvise, like
a map, a threat’s impending doom, or a
complex political web
3)		 What you want to prep, because it
brings you joy, reduces stress, or helps
you meet a goal or grant a player’s wish
Specifically, resist the urge to prep a plot or
every potential path the PCs might take.
Prep what you need, what you can’t improvise, and what helps you be your best. Then
play to find out what happens.

579

The Game Ongoing

Check-ins
Play with any group long enough, and
you’re bound to have issues. Players who
get on each other’s nerves. Mismatched
expectations. Disruptive behaviors. Hurt
feelings. Miscommunications. Etc.
When there’s a player-level issue, talk to
them about it. Don’t let things fester, and
for Tor’s sake don’t try to “fix” the issue
with game mechanics or fictional “consequences” (punishments). There is no ingame solution for a player-level problem.
Stonetop includes tools to facilitate this
kind of communication. Content guidelines (page 66) help set expectations
and boundaries. Reviewing them at the
start of each session (see opposite page)
keeps them top-of-mind. Calling “time
out” gives you a way to course correct
mid-session. And the End of Session
move invites players to ask for things to
change. (And if your group finds other
communication or safety tools to be
helpful, then by all means, use them!)
Some issues, though, are best handled
with a check-in: outside of a session,
one-on-one or with only the relevant
players. Maybe you do this in person,
right after a session, or over a drink, or
on a walk, or whatever. Maybe it’s a call
or an online chat. Pick an approach that
feels comfortable for all involved.
Encourage your players to initiate
check-ins, too, with you and with each
other. If they have a concern, the best
thing to do is talk about it.

580

If you suspect that a player is unhappy,
then let them know what you’ve noticed
and give them space to respond. “Hey,
you seemed uncomfortable during that
scene with Iwan. Did we cross a line
there?” See where the conversation goes.
Sometimes, it’s unrelated to the game,
or you misread the situation, or they just
don’t want to talk about it.
When a player says that they’re unhappy
with something about the game, listen.
Ask for clarification and specifics, but
don’t argue or defend yourself. Thank
them, and ask them what they’d like to
see change.
Can you make or facilitate that change?
If so, great—do it. If you can’t, or don’t
want to, that’s okay too. But be honest
about it. Have a calm conversation about
why. Look for a compromise that works.
If a player is causing trouble, then point
out what they’re doing. Focus on behaviors and impact, not personality. “When
you keep canceling last-minute, I can’t
prep effectively. Or I have to prep more,
in case you’re not there.” Say what you’d
like to see change. Ask if they’re able to
make that change, and how they plan to
do so. Try to find a solution if you can.
Sometimes, the solution is “stop playing
together,” or to stop playing Stonetop and
play something else. That’s okay—you
and your players are more important
than the game! But you’ll never figure
that out without talking to your players.
So check in with each other, often.

The Game Ongoing

Starting
a session

Folks arrive at a session carrying their
week with them—work, kids, traffic, etc.
Give everyone a chance to settle. Chat and
catch up. Let the noise of the world fade a
bit before you start to play.
Have a checklist of things to do at the start
of each session. Once everyone is settled,
start to work through that list. This serves as
a ritual. You’re drawing a magic circle and
stepping back into the fictional world you’ve
made together. Each group’s checklist will
be different, but common items include:

Logistics

What are your plans for food and drink?
Who’s taking notes? When do folks need
to leave? When should you take breaks?
Etc. Set timers as needed. Be sure to save
time for the End of Session move!

Recap

Remind everyone what happened last time,
and where things left off. If possible, get
the players to do this themselves. Ask the
notetaker to give a recap, or ask players to
take turns doing so. Add details and make
corrections as needed, but don’t nitpick.
Listen to what your players say—it’s a good
indicator of what’s important to them!

Content guidelines

As a group, read the “Content” section
on your steading playbook. If you think
this session might bump against these
guidelines, say so now. Ask if anyone wants
to add or change something. Remind folks
to call “time out” if a guideline is broken,
if they need a break, or if they want to talk
things over. See page 18 for guidance.

Highlighting

Go over stuff that you want everyone to
keep in mind or at least be aware of. This
might include:
		PC and/or follower instincts
		Relationship changes from the end of
last session
		Outstanding wishes
		Goals you’re working on, that you’d like
the players to help you with
Customize this list to match your needs
and preferences. Not every group highlights the same things.

Bookkeeping

Have players update their playbooks and
inserts if needed. Like, if they’ve had a
few days at home since the last session,
have them reset HP and clear debilities. If
someone Leveled Up, make sure they’ve
updated their XP and marked a new move.
That sort of thing.

Starting moves,
			questions, love letters

If any PCs have moves that trigger at the
start of session (like the Would-be Hero’s
“Destined” background), or if you’ve written setup questions or love letters, do them
now. Let them serve as a final shift into
actual play.

Opening scene(s)

Address one or more PCs. Say (or ask)
where they are, who they’re with, what
they’re up to. Describe the environment,
ask some questions. Portray NPCs.
Make a soft GM move, something that
propels the PC into action or conflict. Ask,
“What do you do?” Play to find out what
happens.
581

The Game Ongoing

Pacing

Stonetop tends to be slower-paced than many
RPGs. It has its moments of drama and
intense action. But it also encourages you to
dwell on the details of daily life and to spend
time chewing the scenery. Finding the right
pace and rhythm for your group is important.

Adventures vs. homefront
A key tension in the game is how much
time to spend on adventures (expeditions
and crises) vs. homefront play. The game
won’t always tell you when it’s time to
switch, and that’s intentional.

Use your players’ wishes from the End of
Session to calibrate the pace of the game.
If the players are itching for adventure,
nudge them towards letting the Seasons
Change, or offer an opportunity to make
progress towards some goal. If they want
more downtime but the Seasons Change
and a threat worsens or makes itself
known, maybe telegraph a threat that they
can’t do anything about right now (like
growing unrest in Marshedge or some fell
beast spotted in the Flats).
The game mechanics will sometimes drive
play in a particular, unavoidable way. If the
Seasons Change and threats abound, then
there’s no way folks are getting a quiet
winter. But maybe you can give each PC
one pleasant day before the trouble starts?
If your players want to constantly work on
projects or head off on multiple adventures
each season, don’t be afraid to pump the
brakes. Remind them of their obligations,
and if they ignore them, start to oppose
their wishes. Tell them the consequences
of rushing off on a half-baked expedition
with no chance of success. Etc.
582

Increasing the pace

If you’re not getting as much done in a
session as you and your players would like,
then try some of these:
Skip the familiar. If you’ve played out trips
on the West Road before, you don’t need
another montage. Say, “Four days later, you
see the smoke from Gordin’s Delve,” and
move on.
Zoom out. Resist the instinct to play out
every interaction or decision, every setback,
every room of a site. Establish the PCs’
general intentions, then gloss over the obvious, the trivial, the stuff that’s just color.
Resolve a whole course of action with a
single move and some description.
Frame scenes aggressively. Start late, end
early. Have a purpose for every scene you
frame—a question to answer, a conflict to
resolve, something to convey, etc.—and cut
away as soon as that purpose is met. Use
love letters (page 568) to set up strong
opening scenes.
Push towards resolution. Resist the urge
to complicate, escalate, or otherwise draw
out a situation. Have foes flee or surrender
when the fight turns against them. When
you get the chance to make a hard GM
move, end the current situation and push
the PCs into a new one. Don’t put them in
a spot when you can capture them. Don’t
pick a lesser success when they can succeed
with a cost and move on.
Prevent swirl. If the players start to spin
their wheels, intervene. Help them identify
what they want to accomplish, rather than
keep talking about what they might do.
Then lay out some ways to accomplish
their goals. If they keep swirling, make a
GM move that forces them to act.

The Game Ongoing

Advancement

To start, PCs earn enough XP to Level Up
every 2-4 sessions. It takes more sessions
to Level Up as PCs advance—the XP cost
increases, and players roll 6- less often.
A PC can Level Up only at home, which
encourages players to return to Stonetop
once they’ve accumulated enough XP. PCs
can also Burn Brightly at this point, which
lets them exchange excess XP for success
(or fewer complications).

Slowing the rate

Higher-level play

Past level 6, PCs become increasingly
competent and harder to challenge. They
have better stats, more ways of generating
advantage, and lots of tools for overcoming
challenges and mitigating dangers. They
can start to feel invincible.
To continue challenging higher-level PCs:
Up the stakes. Use foes with messy attacks
and moves that inflict lasting harm. Don’t
just threaten the harvest, threaten Stonetop itself. Don’t just threaten the village,
threaten the region as a whole.

Ideally, PCs should Level Up only once
between adventures. You want players to
see each new move in play before they
gain another. If your players often Level
Up multiple times between adventures,
consider some of the following.

Make 6- results hurt. High-level PCs roll
low less often, so go hard when they do.
Capture them. Reveal a betrayal. Destroy a
prized possession. Sever a limb. Kill a loved
one. Be irrevocable.

Reset XP to 0 on Level Up. The simplest
tweak, but some players might hate it. It
also undercuts XP incentives once a PC
has enough to Level Up.

Be aggressive. Keep making soft GM
moves, any chance you get. Make moves
that affect multiple PCs. Make moves that
force them to react before they can do
what they want.

Increase the XP requirement. The default
requirement is 6 + (2 × level). Increase 6 to
a 10 to add an extra session to most levels.
Multiply their current level by 3 instead of
2 to really slow the rate of advancement at
higher levels.
Adjust the End of Session questions. Be
stricter about what counts as a “Yes,” or
rewrite questions to be harder to achieve.
For example, “Did we learn more about the
world or its history?” could become “Did
we unearth any secrets of the past?”
Let players always Burn Brightly, even if
they lack the XP to Level Up. (If you have
a Would-be Hero with the Driven background, you’ll need to tweak that move.)

Hit them where they’re weak. Look for
ways to make them roll their lower stats.
Threaten vulnerable NPCs and resources.
Make them choose where to focus their
efforts, and make those choices matter.
Present threats on multiple fronts. Whatever they don’t deal with should escalate,
go poorly, or have repercussions.
But also—and this is important—let them
enjoy their wins. Give them chances to
steamroll challenges that previously would
have given them pause. Celebrate their
competence and their accomplishments.
Then, when you do challenge them, it’ll
feel all the more dire.
583

The Game Ongoing

Retiring PCs
A PC might exit play because:

		They die and pass through the Last Door
		They retire after a lasting injury, as per
Convalesce (page 249)
		Surplus runs short in winter (page 518),
and the weariest PC’s player has them
die, leave, or retire
		They cross the line from protagonist to
villain (page 587)
		Their player decides to have them leave
Stonetop or retire from adventuring
		Their player leaves the game

Becoming a threat

When a PC descends into villainy and
monstrosity, they stop becoming a PC and
become a threat under the GM’s control.
Write them up as both a threat (page
282) and a monster (page 391), using
their established moves, possessions, and
abilities as inspiration. Don’t worry about
replicating their playbook abilities exactly,
and feel free to make them scarier than
they were as a PC.

Retirement

If a PC retires from play but stays in the
village or the region, then turn them into
an NPC. By default, their portrayal falls to
you. But if everyone agrees, their original
player can portray them.
Whoever portrays them, don’t let them
overshadow the PCs. They’re retired! It’s
okay for them to advise and support the
PCs, or to help meet the requirements of a
plan. But they shouldn’t act as followers or
solve the PCs’ problems. If need be, ask the
ex-PC’s original player why they can’t or
won’t help with any given problem.
584

Epilogues

When a PC retires or passes away, give
them an epilogue: a scene or two to send
them off and say goodbye.
If the PC died, play out the funeral or
wake. Establish the local traditions, if you
haven’t already. Show how NPCs react.
Ask the surviving PCs to say some words.
Let the dead PC’s player savor the farewell
to their fallen alter ego.
If the PC leaves Stonetop, then have the
player narrate their departure, as well as
a glimpse of their future—a “Where are
they now?” moment. On the road to Lygos,
wandering among the Hillfolk, stepping
on the Fae paths to never be seen again…
That sort of thing.
If the PC retires, play out their decision to
do so. Then, now or later, have the player
describe a typical day in their new life.
Who are they with and what are they up
to? How do they seem to be getting on?
What do they seem to be enjoying? What
regrets weigh them down?
Invite the other players to contribute, by
asking questions, suggesting details, or
playing out their own scenes of grief and
loss, of absence and new beginnings.
Epilogues should skip game mechanics.
These aren’t scenes with conflict or even
stakes. They’re just vignettes, meant to give
a sense of closure to a PC’s tale.

The Game Ongoing

New PCs

You might need to introduce a new PC to
an ongoing game when:
		A new player joins your group
		A PC dies, retires, or otherwise
leaves play (see previous page), and the
player creates a replacement PC
If you’re adding a player to an ongoing
game, then brief them on the game so far.
Have them read the Setting Overview and
Moves & Gear handouts. Ideally, have
them read the group’s session logs and look
over the steading playbook. Schedule time
to answer any questions they have.

Creating the character

Have the player pick one of the unused
playbooks. They should also pick a background that reflects either a new arrival
in Stonetop or a local NPC stepping up
to PC status—each playbook has at least
two such backgrounds. You don’t want this
new, highly-skilled PC to have been here
all along without ever getting involved.
When the player gets to the “worldbuilding” part of their playbook (back page, left
column), help them make choices that fit
with the established lore of your game.
As a rule, new PCs start play at level 1,
no matter how seasoned everyone else is.
Don’t worry about “balance.” A level 1 PC
in Stonetop is plenty competent, and starting fresh lets the player learn the playbook
and develop their character through play.
That said: it’s okay to start a new PC at a
higher level if there’s a compelling reason
(like the PC is an ancient hero freed from
stasis), the player is familiar with Stonetop,
and the whole group is cool with it.

Introductions

Have the player stop at the Introductions
part of their playbook. Decide together
how you’ll bring them into the game.
There are two basic approaches:
Play it out. Introduce the new PC in play.
Frame a scene with them arriving in town,
or have them be there when the PCs get
home from an adventure. Or, like, have
that clumsy kid get struck by lightning
during a crisis and become the Stormmarked Heavy just in time to save the day.
Skip the formal Introductions on their
playbook, but ask questions as needed.
This approach can be fun and visceral, but
it can also take up a lot of game-time and
inadvertently set up PC-PC conflicts.
Time skip. The new PC appears (or steps
up) between sessions, during a stretch of
downtime. Establish that they’ve been here
(or been active) a while. Have the new PC
do Introductions (page 56) more or less
normally at the start of the next session
(or between sessions, if that works for your
group). Maybe write a love letter (page
568) to each existing PC that both covers
events during the time skip and that establishes their connection to this new PC.

Getting them involved

Once they’re in play, make sure the new
PC matters. Prepare hooks that touch on
their background, instincts, or unresolved
questions. Work their worldbuilding elements into the game. Give them a little
more spotlight, and allow them to find
their place in the game.
Handled well, the introduction of a new
PC is not disruptive. It deepens and adds
new texture to your game.

585

The Game Ongoing

Conflict
between PCs

One of your players’ agendas is to portray
a compelling character (page 37), and
such characters won’t always agree. That’s
okay. Actually, that’s great! Handled well,
conflict between PCs can be high drama
and some of the most memorable parts of
the game.
Most PC conflicts are just disagreements,
or one PC trying to stop another PC from
doing something rash. The Would-be Hero
goes to charge the corrupted boar, but the
Marshal Interferes and stops them. The
Blessed wants to subdue the beast and heal
it if she can, and Persuades the other PCs
to let her try.

586

Encourage players to argue in character,
not to “win” but to establish their positions
and their values. Use moves like Persuade
(vs. PCs), Interfere, and Seek Insight to
keep things moving.

Intractable differences

Over time, disagreements can grow into
ongoing conflicts. The Judge won’t stand
for the Fox’s deceit. The Fox can’t forgive
the Heavy for his husband’s death. The
Lightbearer frets over the state of the
Seeker’s soul.
When you first sense such tensions, check
in with the players. Make sure that the
frustrations and emotions are felt by the
characters and not the players. If the players are getting upset with each other, take
a step back and look for a solution. Update

The Game Ongoing

your content guidelines, fix the fiction
if you need to, and commit to whatever
changes you’ve all agreed upon.
If everyone is on board, though, then lean
into the conflict. Ask folks what they’re
looking for. Escalation? Resolution?
Heartbreak? Use their answers to guide
what comes next.

Open conflict

When PCs directly oppose each other—
through violence, subterfuge, sabotage, or
whatever—then slow down. A lot.
First, do a temperature check. If things feel
heated between the players, then call time
out. Check in. Make sure everyone wants
to proceed. A little conversation can go a
long way towards preventing hurt feelings,
resentment, and distrust.
If everyone is on board with the conflict,
then give everyone involved a chance to
declare their intent. When one PC does
something that actively affects another PC
or their interests, tell the targeted player
what they perceive. Ask what they do in
response. If any other PCs are present or
involved, ask what they do as well. Figure
out what moves are being triggered, and in
what order. Very often, someone will end
up Interfering.
Use the results of PC actions and player
moves to inform what comes next. Keep
checking in with the players. Move the
spotlight and give other PCs chances to
get involved. If someone rolls a 6-, push
things to their logical (if unfortunate)
conclusion, or interrupt the whole conflict
with some unrelated but pressing problem.

Descent into villainy

Sometimes, a PC starts down a dark path.
Maybe they keep using a major arcanum
and racking up consequences. Maybe they
are corrupted by the Things Below. Maybe
they die but refuse to pass on, and linger
as a Ghost (page 148), Revenant (page
150), or Thrall (page 152). Maybe they
just make one grim choice after another.
It’s usually okay for a PC to be wayward or
wicked, but not both. If a PC schemes, lies,
and causes trouble for the other PCs but
their heart is in the right place, that’s interesting. And if a PC relies on foul magic
or despicable acts, but does so in alignment
with the other PCs’ goals, then that can be
compelling. But a PC who’s both evil and
opposed to the other PCs is no longer a
protagonist. They’re a villain.
When a PC crosses the line into villainy,
or just cozies up to it, check in. Does this
PC’s player think they can be redeemed?
Do the other players want to try? If not,
does everyone want to play out one last
conflict or scheme? If the answer to any of
these is “No,” then the PC should become
a threat (page 584). If the answer to all
three questions is “Yes,” then focus on that
confrontation in an upcoming session. If
the party fails to redeem their one-time
ally, then that PC becomes a threat.

Regarding secrets

When PCs come into conflict, it’s natural
that they will start lying to each other, or
keeping secrets. Great! More drama! But
don’t let players keep secrets from each
other. Don’t accept notes they pass you.
Don’t have private chats or huddles in the
hall. Encourage everyone to keep in mind
what they know vs. what their character
knows, and then enjoy the dramatic irony.
587

The Game Ongoing

Time skips

Stonetop often has you gloss over stretches
of time. You might cover days, weeks, or
months of travel or downtime with little
more than a brief description and a few
questions for the PCs. But sometimes, you
want to zoom way out and jump farther
forward in time. Maybe you reach the end
of a story arc and you’d like to let things
breathe, or let the PCs age and mature.
Perhaps your group takes a break for
real-world reasons, and you want to reflect
that in the fiction. A PC might head off to
Lygos and you want to jump ahead to their
return. Or maybe there’s a big shake-up
(page 590) and you want to skip the immediate aftermath.
How you handle a time skip depends
largely on its length.
588

Months, seasons, years

If you’re skipping a few months, seasons,
or years, then write love letters (page
568) to cover the gap. These can be fairly
involved, including not only a roll and
its results, but also invitations to narrate
events and/or to involve the other players.
“Dear Marshal, it’s been a peaceful year but
tell us—what trouble has stemmed from
having a standing watch that you never
predicted? Then, ask each of the other PCs
one of the following …”
If the time skip straddles the change of
just one season, have the players make the
Seasons Change move and incorporate
its results into your letters. For a skip that
spans multiple seasons, work changes to
steading elements (Fortunes, Surplus,
improvements, etc.) into the love letters.
Either way, prep a new adventure (page
251) to bring things back with a bang.

The Game Ongoing

Up to a decade or two

If you’re skipping more than a few years,
up to a decade or two, take a session off
from actual play. Instead, discuss:
		 How long, exactly, are you skipping?
		 Who wants to continue playing their
current PC? How might they change?
		 Who wants to make new PCs?
		 What NPCs and setting elements
should continue to be important?
		 How has the setting changed?
Have anyone who wants to play a new PC
create them now, picking any background
that makes sense given the time skip. Do
Introductions (page 56) as normal, with
the existing PCs each taking turns during
steps 6-7 (PC connections). Pick a starting
season and do the Seasons Change move.
Use the result to craft a First Adventure
(page 251), writing love letters to existing
PCs to help fill in the skipped-over time.

Generations

If you jump ahead generations, then you’re
starting a whole new game. Hold a new
first session (page 32). Discuss how the
setting has changed in light of the game
that just ended. Make and introduce new
PCs, keeping those changes in mind. Ask
questions about the timeline between the
prior game and this one. Have spring burst
forth, and prep a first adventure.
In play, continue to ask questions and fill in
the timeline between games. Sprinkle this
game with connections to the prior one.
Encourage players to do the same. Revisit
the ruin where the previous Heavy died.
Reveal that this Fox’s father was that kid
who tagged along after the old PCs. Gasp
when the Marshal anoints her crew with
red pigment, to honor the scar borne by
the last generation’s Would-be Hero.
589

The Game Ongoing

Shake-ups

The PCs all die, or leave Stonetop for
good. The village is razed and its people
scattered. A cataclysm is unleashed upon
the world. Big changes like these can
upend your game. Don’t panic. Talk to the
players about how they’d like to proceed.
You have two basic options:

Option 1: End the game

The right answer is often the simplest: call
it quits. If the story feels complete, or the
shake-up has stolen the game’s momentum, then let the game end. Consider using
the Denouement (page 595) move if that
kind of closure feels right.
Take a break. Play something else for a
while. You can always come back to Stonetop later, either with a whole new game or
by doing a time skip and starting up a few
decades or generations later.

Option 2: Pivot

Keep playing, adjusting the game to reflect
the shake-up. Skip forward an appropriate
amount of time (see previous page). Make
any mechanical changes you need. The
specifics will vary case by case.
Pivots like this can be hard. They make you
rethink the game’s core assumptions. You
have to update rules and handouts. You’re
basically designing a new game. But if you
and your players are down for it, it can be
rewarding and fun as hell.

590

The PCs have left Stonetop to help allied
Hillfolk clans in a war against their
rivals. I write up a new steading playbook for the ally’s war-camp, skip ahead
a few months, and write love letters to
establish the new situation. The next few
seasons might involve stealth missions,
attempts to secure resources, and pitched
battles. When the war ends and the
PCs head home, I’ll write love letters to
determine what happened while they
were gone.
The PCs have all died trying to stop the
release of Y’aaw’kara (Book II, page
421). A new ice age is unleashed and
the Howling Curse plagues the land. We
decide to skip ahead a decade or so and
start fresh with all new PCs, and discuss
how Stonetop has adapted to the harsh
new climate, updating its resources and
potential improvements accordingly.
I rewrite the Seasons Change move,
update the inventory inserts and gear
lists, and give everyone a custom insert
for tracking the Howling Curse (Book
II, page 492).
The PCs have been exiled from Stonetop
and have taken up lives as wandering
adventurers. The steading playbook will
no longer be relevant to them; most of
the Homefront moves no longer apply,
or would be much harder to trigger, so I
rewrite some of the questions in the End
of Session move, and update Level Up to
require “a quiet stretch of downtime,” instead of “a quiet stretch at home.” All in
all, the game will now play out more like
a traditional fantasy-adventure RPG.

The Game Ongoing

Ending
your game

All stories end. Maybe the story of your
Stonetop burns bright and fast. Maybe it
stretches on for years.
Maybe it just… fizzles out. And that’s fine.
Not every story gets a good ending.
Ideally, though, you’ll see the end coming. Maybe real life forces your hand, and
one or more of your players are moving, or
having a kid, or starting school or a new
job and they can’t play anymore. Maybe
you sense the game’s story arcing towards
its conclusion. Or maybe you or some of
your players are just getting the itch to play
something else.

Planning for
the end

When you see the end
coming, you have an opportunity. An opportunity
for climax, denouement,
and a fitting farewell. A
chance to give your shared story the final
chapter it deserves.

1 		Schedule a planning session
						 Once you realize it’s time to start
ending the game, schedule a planning
session with your players. Expect it to take
an hour or two, so it might just be part of a
normal session.
Try to make sure everyone can be there. If
you can, give yourself some lead-time so
that you and your players can prep.

2 		Prepare
						 Ask your players to review the questions below, and give them some thought
before the planning session. If they can’t
attend, ask them to send you their wish lists
beforehand. Encourage everyone else to
come with notes, or at least thoughts.
Before the game ends:
		 What arcs do you want resolved?
		 What questions do you want to see
answered?
		 What scenes do you hope to have?
		 Are there any moves, artifacts, or
arcana you’d love to see in action?
		 What goals do you want a chance for
your PC to accomplish?
If you have time, provide the players with
a list of hanging threads for reference. That
includes active threats (page 277), stuff
from your “I wonder…” list (page 33),
unsolved mysteries and unfished projects,
player wishes yet to be granted, and so forth.
Give these questions some thought, yourself. You’re allowed to have opinions!

3 		Assemble the wishlist
						Once you’ve gathered for the planning session, refer to the questions from
step 2, above. Print them out if playing in
person, or post them somewhere everyone
can see. The goal here is to establish each
player’s wishlist of things they’d like to do,
resolve, or see in play before the game ends.
If anyone is absent and sent you their wish
list, read it off now. After that, go around
the group and have each player share their
list. Ask for clarification if needed, but try
to limit discussion. You want everyone to
freely share what they want.
Take notes, ideally somewhere public.
591

The Game Ongoing

Keep track of which players wished for
what. If you have ideas about how you’ll
accomplish these things, great—maybe jot
that down privately. But keep the focus on
what folks want for now.
If you have any of your own wishes that
haven’t been mentioned, share those now.
Open the floor for discussion. Let folks ask
questions, make suggestions, endorse other
folk’s wishes, and reconsider their own. If
your players wish for conflicting things,
talk it over and come to a compromise.
If they wish for something infeasible in
the time you have, try to identify what is
feasible instead.
Prioritize. Everyone (including you) can
pick one “must-have” item, and up to two
“important” items. Give folks the chance to
strike off items they added earlier that they
don’t actually care about. Everything else is
low priority, a “nice to have.”
To be clear: this is a wish list, not a list of
promises. You’re all agreeing to try and
fit these things in the game, but there’s
no guarantee how exactly they’ll play out
when that happens. “Deal with Brennan
& the trade embargo” just means a chance
to tie off those threads. Hopefully the PCs
will succeed. But they might not!

Driving toward resolution
From now on, push play towards those
must-have and important wishes. Work
them into your prep. Keep the wish list
handy as a reference during play, and get to
the “nice to have” wishes as you can.
Make use of techniques for increasing the
pace (page 582). Skip over the familiar.
Zoom out and gloss over stuff where you
can. Frame scenes aggressively. Make GM
592

I’m starting a new job soon, and won’t
be able to keep running the game. I
reckon we have time for maybe four
more sessions. Prior to the next session,
I send out the questions and my list of
hanging threads, and ask folks to create
wishlists. During our planning session,
our discussion results in these priorities:
MUST-HAVES
•			 Deal with Brennan & the trade
embargo (Rhianna, me)
•			 See the Mighty Servant in play
(Vahid)
•			 Tell Caradoc how I feel (Blodwen)
•			 Prove himself to the village, once
and for all (Caradoc)
IMPORTANT
•			 Resolve Nia’s spider connection
(me, Blodwen)
•			 Try to make peace with Owain
(Rhianna)
•			 Settle things with Maire (Vahid)
•			 Use Big Damn Hero (Caradoc)
•			 Have it out with Vahid (Rhianna)
NICE TO HAVE
•			 Tegwen gets her comeuppance
(Blodwen, Rhianna)
•			 Get back at Mutra (Vahid)
•			 See Eira in retirement (Rhianna)
•			 Morwenna’s wedding (Caradoc)
•			 Punch Brennan’s face (Caradoc)
moves that resolve situations, rather than
escalating or drawing them out. Resist the
urge to introduce new threads or complications unless they directly relate to an item
on the wish list. Step in when the players
start to swirl.
Focus. You’re in the endgame, now.

The Game Ongoing

If we’re going to see the Mighty Servant
on-screen, Vahid will need to acquire its
aetherium helm and spend time with its
chassis, assembling it. Dealing with Brennan and the trade embargo implies a direct
confrontation. And if Caradoc is going to
prove himself to the village, it should happen near Stonetop.
We’ve got only two sessions left, so we jump
to spring and I use love letters to set the
scene: Morwenna’s wedding (a subdued
affair), Blodwen and Caradoc dancing,
Brennan’s goons blockading the West Road,
a skeevy adventurer sneaking past to sell the
aetherium helm to Vahid.
During the session, Rhianna and Vahid
have a row over his obsession with the Servant. The PCs and Rhianna’s crew kill most
of Brennan’s men and drive off the rest.

Caradoc uses Big Damn Hero and crosses
“Would-be” off his playbook. Vahid heads
north to assemble the Mighty Servant. The
session ends there.
For the final session, we skip ahead to summer. Another round of love letters establish
that Vahid is still assembling the Servant.
Blodwen’s and Caradoc’s romance is going
well. And Brennan is marching towards
Stonetop with a force of Manmarcher
mercenaries, intent on beating Stonetop
into submission.
Will Rhianna and Caradoc rally the village? Will Blodwen convince Nia to call
on the spiders for aid? Will Vahid return
with the Mighty Servant and help turn
the tables? Will Caradoc punch Brennan in
the face?
I don’t know, but we’ll play to find out!
593

The Game Ongoing

The final sessions

Figure out when your last session will be,
and how many sessions you have before
then. As you prep for those final sessions,
keep the wishlist forefront in your mind.
Look at what you need to cover and how
many sessions you have left. Use time
skips (page 588), love letters (page
568), and aggressive scene-framing to get
the wishes on screen. If you don’t see a way
to do so, talk to your players and ask for
ideas. If you think you have an idea, but it
involves committing the PCs to a certain
course of action, check in and make sure
everyone is on board.

594

Ignore moves, procedures, and rules that
don’t help you hit the wish list items in
the time that remains. If the last session
will hinge on a battle with Yaarowslow the
Many in the ruins of Vor Svetelik, then
don’t bother with Seasons Change or the
journey there or how many supplies the
PCs have left.
During the finale, especially: go big and
don’t hold back. Make bold GM moves
that force the PCs to make hard choices,
take desperate risks, and put it all on the
line. When they roll a 6-, make it hurt.
Now more than ever, let things burn.
But also: be a fan of the player characters.
Encourage boldness. Present them with
opportunities. Give them all a chance
to be heroes, one last time.

The Game Ongoing

The end

When the final conflict ends and the dust
starts to settle, take a breath. Then wrap up
the current situation to whatever level of
detail makes sense.
Don’t use any moves or mechanics. Just
describe the scene and give the players
space to say what they do, what happens
next. If there are moments they want to
play out, let them. If they have questions,
answer them.
Use what time remains to do this one last
move, together.

DENOUEMENT
When the game comes to an end, go
around the table. On your turn, pick a
scene below and ask the player or the
GM to describe it.
When you describe a scene, make it
involve your PC, an NPC important
to them, or (if you’re the GM) any
NPC you like.
		A return home
		A funeral, a farewell, an ending
		A struggle, an absence, grief
		A time of rebuilding, renewal, hope
		A lasting change, a transition
		A beginning, a fresh start, a birth
		A memory, a story, a legend
Go around as many times as you like.
Change who you ask each time, or
just pick a scene to describe yourself.
When you’re done, thank your friends,
wish them well, and say goodbye.
The end.

caveat emptor

All of the above are relevant and potentially useful as inspiration, but they’re not necessarily all good.
And some of the creators held and espoused hurtful beliefs, which permeates their work. Others have
done or been accused of terrible things. People, like art and our consumption of it, are complicated.
